Use RabbitMQ for bounded messaging and dispatch, not as a hidden business-process engine.
Validate dead-letter, retry, and poison-message handling deliberately.

stacks.name: Yjs
strengths: CRDT model is effective for collaborative editors and shared state where concurrent local updates must merge without central locking.
Good fit when collaboration semantics are a product feature rather than incidental websocket plumbing.
Works well with managed transport layers when the app needs both shared state and realtime presence.
tradeoffs: CRDTs add conceptual complexity and state-debugging overhead; they are not the right answer for every realtime app.
A collaboration-state library does not remove the need to design permissions, persistence, and transport boundaries.
best_for: Collaborative editors, shared cursors, multi-user documents, and stateful realtime collaboration.
Teams that need deterministic merge behavior under concurrent edits.
avoid_when: The product only needs notifications, presence, or simple pubsub without shared structured state.
The team is not prepared to reason about merge semantics and persisted collaboration state.
best_practices: Keep transport, persistence, and permission logic explicit instead of treating CRDT state as the whole architecture.
Test merge and reconnect behavior under concurrent offline edits, not only ideal websocket sessions.
Use CRDTs when shared structured state matters, not for simple event fanout.

stacks.name: Rust
strengths: Strong fit for performance-sensitive tooling, native integrations, and reliability-focused command or desktop backends.
Cargo ecosystem gives cohesive dependency, build, and test workflows for Rust-first projects.
Works well as the systems layer behind Tauri, CLIs, and selected service components.
tradeoffs: Compile times and ownership-model learning curve are real costs for teams without prior Rust experience.
Not every scripting or internal-automation use case benefits enough to justify the tooling shift.
best_for: Native tooling, desktop backends, CLIs, and performance-sensitive libraries or services.
Teams that want strong compile-time guarantees in operationally critical code.
avoid_when: The team needs the fastest path for simple scripting or glue-code automation.
Project requirements do not justify the tooling and language transition cost.
best_practices: Keep workspace structure, linting, and release profile conventions explicit from the start.
Use Rust where correctness or systems integration really matters instead of forcing it into simple scripting roles.
Treat cargo test, clippy, and formatting as baseline quality gates, not optional polish.

stacks.name: .NET
strengths: Coherent SDK and CLI make it effective for enterprise services, internal tools, and desktop applications on mature build pipelines.
Strong fit when teams need a well-supported compiled platform with long-term enterprise tooling and packaging paths.
Works well for cross-platform services and Windows-heavy desktop or internal-business workloads.
tradeoffs: The ecosystem is excellent when embraced coherently, but mixing ad hoc project styles weakens that advantage.
Less attractive for teams that are already deeply standardized on Python or TypeScript and do not need compiled-platform benefits.
best_for: Enterprise services, desktop applications, and internal tooling with stable build and packaging expectations.
Teams that benefit from a mature SDK-driven build and test workflow.
avoid_when: The project only needs lightweight scripting or extremely rapid prototype iteration.
The team does not benefit enough from the compiled-platform and tooling model.
best_practices: Keep SDK versioning and solution structure explicit so CI and local builds stay aligned.
Use the dotnet CLI and consistent test/build commands rather than IDE-only workflows.
Treat packaging, signing, and runtime version policy as part of platform ownership.

stacks.name: Terraform
strengths: Write/Plan/Apply workflow: declare desired state in HCL, preview changes with plan, then apply.
Providers for AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, GitHub, Datadog, Splunk, and thousands more via Terraform Registry.
State file tracks real infrastructure as source of truth for drift detection and dependency resolution.
Modules enable reusable, composable infrastructure components shareable across teams.
Parallel resource creation via dependency graph: independent resources provisioned simultaneously.
HCP Terraform for team collaboration: remote state, policy enforcement, and audit logging.
Immutable infrastructure approach: replace rather than upgrade reduces snowflake configuration drift.
tradeoffs: State file is sensitive; requires secure remote backend (S3+DynamoDB, HCP Terraform) for team use.
Provider ecosystem quality varies; some providers lag behind cloud provider API releases.
Plan/apply cycle adds latency compared to direct SDK calls; not suitable for imperative provisioning logic.
best_for: Multi-cloud or hybrid cloud infrastructure declared as version-controlled code.
Teams that need infrastructure drift detection, plan-before-apply safety, and audit trails.
Provisioning repeatable environments (dev/staging/prod) from shared module libraries.
avoid_when: Infrastructure changes are highly dynamic or require complex imperative logic (use Pulumi or CDK).
A single cloud provider with strong managed IaC tooling reduces cognitive overhead for the team.
best_practices: Always run terraform plan before apply in CI/CD; never apply without reviewing the plan output.
Store state in a remote backend (S3+DynamoDB or HCP Terraform); never commit state files to git.
Use workspaces or directory-per-environment patterns for dev/staging/prod isolation.
Pin provider versions in required_providers to prevent breaking changes from provider updates.
Use terraform fmt and terraform validate in pre-commit hooks for consistent HCL style.
Use modules for reusable patterns; publish to internal module registry for team-wide reuse.

stacks.name: Supabase
strengths: Full-stack platform: PostgreSQL + Auth + Storage + Realtime + Edge Functions + pgvector in one service.
Row Level Security (RLS) on all PostgreSQL tables provides per-row access control at the database layer.
Auth module supports email/password, OAuth (GitHub, Google, etc.), magic link, and mobile providers.
Realtime subscriptions to PostgreSQL change events; broadcast and presence channels.
Storage with RLS access policies for file and media management.
AI & Vectors module: pgvector integration with built-in embedding generation and similarity search via REST.
Queues (pgmq) and Cron (pg_cron) modules for async jobs and scheduled tasks without separate infrastructure.
REST API and GraphQL API auto-generated from schema; Python, JS, Flutter, Swift, Kotlin clients.
Self-hostable via Docker for data residency and compliance requirements.
tradeoffs: Supabase client libraries abstract direct PostgreSQL access; complex queries may need raw SQL via RPC.
Self-hosting requires maintaining multiple interconnected Supabase services.
Vendor lock-in risk: while PostgreSQL is portable, Supabase Auth and Storage have proprietary APIs.
best_for: Full-stack web and mobile apps needing auth, database, storage, and real-time without separate services.
Rapid product prototyping where a managed PostgreSQL backend reduces infrastructure overhead.
Teams that need pgvector AI search integrated directly with relational data and RLS policies.
avoid_when: Complex relational schemas require direct PostgreSQL tooling not exposed by Supabase abstractions.
Data residency requirements conflict with available Supabase cloud regions and self-hosting overhead is prohibitive.
best_practices: Enable RLS on every table from the start; disable only for internal service tables with explicit justification.
Use supabase-py with the anon key for public client access; service_role key only in secure server contexts.
Define database changes as SQL migrations in supabase/migrations/ for reproducible schema evolution.
Use Supabase local dev (supabase start) for consistent local development and migration testing.
Use pgvector via the AI & Vectors module for semantic search embedded directly in PostgreSQL RLS policies.
Never expose the service_role key to client-side code; always use per-user JWT tokens for client auth.

stacks.name: React Native
strengths: Shared React mental model reduces context switching for web teams moving into mobile delivery.
Large ecosystem for auth, analytics, navigation, push, and error-reporting integrations.
Can share business logic and selected UI primitives with web stacks when boundaries are intentional.
tradeoffs: Native module dependencies still require iOS and Android build-chain literacy.
Performance-sensitive screens and platform-specific UX often need native escape hatches.
best_for: Product teams already invested in React or TypeScript that need one cross-platform mobile surface.
Consumer or internal mobile apps where one shared product team owns both platforms.
avoid_when: The product needs extremely deep platform-specific UX or device integration from day one.
The team cannot support the native iOS and Android toolchains behind the JavaScript layer.
best_practices: Keep platform-specific modules isolated behind explicit boundaries instead of scattering conditionals through UI code.
Validate navigation, deep-link, push, and offline states on both platforms; emulator parity is not enough.
Treat release, signing, and store submission as first-class delivery concerns, not late-stage chores.

stacks.name: Expo
strengths: Managed workflow reduces native build friction for common mobile app delivery paths.
Expo Router, EAS Build, EAS Update, and dev-client workflows give React Native teams a more coherent mobile platform.
Good default when the team wants over-the-air updates and store builds without designing a native toolchain from scratch.
tradeoffs: Managed workflows still have edges where custom native work forces a prebuild or bare workflow shift.
Teams can underestimate release-channel, signing, and environment management complexity because local startup is simple.
best_for: Cross-platform mobile products led by JavaScript teams that want the fastest path to shipped iOS and Android builds.
Apps that benefit from OTA updates, opinionated mobile project structure, and streamlined CI/CD.
avoid_when: The product needs unsupported native modules or deep platform customization very early.
The team wants a fully native-first build process and is not looking for managed workflow constraints.
best_practices: Use EAS profiles to separate preview, internal, and production distribution cleanly.
Treat runtime config and secrets separately; public app config is not the same thing as server-side secrets.
Exercise a full store-ready build path early, including signing and native config validation.

stacks.name: Flutter
strengths: Single rendering stack produces highly consistent UI across platforms.
Strong fit for product teams that want one shared codebase across mobile and selected desktop surfaces.
Good performance characteristics for animation-heavy or brand-sensitive interfaces.
tradeoffs: Dart introduces a separate language and ecosystem for teams centered on Python or TypeScript.
Platform conventions can be harder to honor if the team forces one visual system everywhere.
best_for: Cross-platform products that prioritize UI consistency and one rendering model over web-stack reuse.
Teams willing to adopt Dart to get mobile and desktop reach from one codebase.
avoid_when: The team wants direct reuse of React or web frontend talent and code.
The product needs a narrowly native-first implementation rather than a shared rendering strategy.
best_practices: Choose Flutter deliberately for rendering consistency, not because it promises zero platform decisions.
Keep native service integrations isolated and documented so the Dart layer stays portable.
Test performance and bundle size on representative target devices before committing fully.

stacks.name: Firebase
strengths: Strong mobile-oriented backend surface spanning auth, analytics, push, crash reporting, remote config, and hosted data services.
Well-suited to mobile products that need a fast path to identity, notifications, instrumentation, and device-facing services.
Broad client SDK coverage for React Native, Flutter, iOS, Android, and web.
tradeoffs: Can pull product architecture toward a provider-centric model quickly if data and auth boundaries are not explicit.
Firestore and other managed abstractions reduce setup friction but can obscure long-term data-shape and portability costs.
best_for: Mobile-first products needing authentication, messaging, analytics, and realtime capabilities quickly.
Cross-platform apps where one hosted backend surface should cover iOS, Android, and some web clients.
avoid_when: The product needs relational-first modeling and direct SQL ownership as a primary design constraint.
The team wants to avoid strong managed-service coupling across auth, events, and app instrumentation.
best_practices: Separate client-safe config from privileged admin credentials; mobile config values are public by design.
Model notification, analytics, and crash-reporting as product capabilities with ownership, not incidental SDK installs.
Validate offline sync, auth state restoration, and device token rotation early in development.

stacks.name: HashiCorp Vault
strengths: Centralized secret storage, dynamic credentials, and policy-driven access reduce credential sprawl across services and pipelines.
Useful when regulated or multi-team environments need auditable secret access rather than scattered environment-file practices.
Dynamic secrets and leased credentials are strong for databases and short-lived machine access.
tradeoffs: Operational overhead is real; Vault is not justified just because plain env vars feel unsophisticated.
Poor policy design or broad root-token workflows undermine most of the security value quickly.
best_for: Teams that need centralized secret management, short-lived credentials, and audited access paths.
Regulated delivery environments where secret handling must be explicit and reviewable.
avoid_when: A small product can safely use a simpler managed secret store owned by its platform provider.
The team is not prepared to operate and govern policy-driven secret workflows.
best_practices: Prefer short-lived credentials and machine identities over long-lived static secrets whenever the target system supports it.
Design policies per service and environment boundary; avoid shared broad tokens that erase audit value.
Treat bootstrap and break-glass access as controlled operational processes, not ad hoc shell commands.

stacks.name: WebAuthn and passkeys
strengths: Phishing-resistant authentication model reduces password reset burden and credential-stuffing exposure.
Strong fit for consumer and workforce login flows when device-bound or synced authenticators are acceptable.
Complements hosted identity providers that expose WebAuthn or passkey enrollment as part of login policy.
tradeoffs: Recovery, fallback, and device enrollment flows still need careful product design; passkeys are not a magic UX shortcut.
Server verification, origin handling, and authenticator policy details can create subtle implementation mistakes if abstracted poorly.
best_for: Products that want phishing-resistant sign-in and reduced password dependence.
Admin, regulated, or high-risk login surfaces where stronger user authentication materially matters.
avoid_when: The product cannot support clear recovery and step-up flows for users who lose device access.
The team is treating passkeys as a replacement for authorization, session review, or device trust decisions.
best_practices: Design recovery and fallback paths explicitly so passkey rollout does not strand legitimate users.
Use passkeys as part of a broader authentication policy that still includes session review, risk checks, and authorization boundaries.
Validate origin, relying-party IDs, and device enrollment assumptions during rollout and testing.

stacks.name: pnpm
install_methods: Install pnpm via Corepack or the official standalone installer for your platform.
Prefer workspace-aware installation through the repo's declared packageManager metadata or Corepack-managed version.
notes: pnpm is a strong default for Node/TypeScript repos that care about deterministic installs, shared dependency storage, and workspace ergonomics.
Keep workspace and lockfile ownership explicit so monorepo tooling does not drift into package-manager confusion.
scaffolding_notes: NEVER write exact pinned versions in package.json (e.g. '14.2.3'). Always use semver ranges such as '^15'. Run 'npm show <package> version' or 'pnpm show <package> version' to confirm the current major before writing the file.

Pinned exact versions prevent automatic pickup of security patches. JavaScript framework ecosystems (Next.js, Nuxt, Remix, SvelteKit, etc.) release security patches as patch versions within a major — a pinned version will never receive them.
Run 'pnpm install' immediately after writing package.json — do not leave it for the user.

stacks.name: npm
install_methods: npm is distributed with Node.js and is available after installing a supported Node runtime.
Use the repo lockfile and engines/packageManager metadata to keep CI and local installs aligned.
notes: npm remains the baseline package-manager surface for many JavaScript projects and should still be represented explicitly in the KB.
Prefer one package manager per repo; mixed npm and pnpm workflows create avoidable lockfile and CI drift.
scaffolding_notes: NEVER write exact pinned versions in package.json (e.g. '14.2.3'). Always use semver ranges such as '^15'. Run 'npm show <package> version' to confirm the current major before writing the file.

Pinned exact versions prevent automatic pickup of security patches. JavaScript framework ecosystems (Next.js, Nuxt, Remix, SvelteKit, etc.) release security patches as patch versions within a major — a pinned version will never receive them.
Run 'npm install' immediately after writing package.json — do not leave it for the user.

stacks.name: Turborepo
install_methods: Install Turborepo in the repo as a dev dependency or bootstrap from the official create flow.
Use it with a workspace-aware package manager so cache and task graph behavior stay coherent.
notes: Turborepo is a strong default for JS/TS monorepos that need repeatable task graphs and remote/local caching.
Model outputs, dependencies, and cache boundaries carefully or the monorepo graph becomes opaque instead of faster.

stacks.name: Nx
install_methods: Install Nx in the repo or create a workspace from the official Nx bootstrap flow.
Prefer a single workspace graph and plugin strategy instead of layering multiple orchestration tools together.
notes: Nx is effective when monorepo teams need graph-aware task execution, affected builds, and stronger project boundary tooling.
Use Nx deliberately as the workspace orchestrator rather than mixing overlapping monorepo abstractions.

stacks.name: cargo
install_methods: cargo is installed with rustup and the standard Rust toolchain.
Use rustup to pin or manage toolchain channels explicitly across local and CI environments.
notes: cargo is the canonical build, test, and packaging surface for Rust projects and Tauri backends.
Treat the toolchain version as part of the repo contract so local and CI builds do not drift.

stacks.name: dotnet CLI
install_methods: Install a supported .NET SDK from the official Microsoft distribution for your platform.
Use global.json when the repo needs a fixed SDK contract across local and CI environments.
notes: dotnet CLI is the baseline build, restore, and test surface for .NET projects and should not be hidden behind IDE-only workflows.
Keep SDK version policy explicit so generated or maintained repos stay reproducible.

stacks.name: pre-commit
strengths: Multi-language pre-commit hook manager: one tool coordinates hooks across all project languages.
.pre-commit-config.yaml declares repos, hooks, and revision pins for reproducible hook configuration.
pre-commit install sets up git hooks; pre-commit run --all-files for CI full-repo validation.
pre-commit autoupdate keeps hook revisions current with upstream releases.
Hook stages cover pre-commit, pre-push, commit-msg, prepare-commit-msg, and post-checkout.
Local hooks for repo-specific scripts without publishing a separate hook package.
CI integration: GitHub Actions, GitLab, CircleCI, and Azure Pipelines all have pre-commit patterns.
tradeoffs: Adds per-commit latency; slow hooks (mypy, full test suite) should be moved to pre-push or CI only.
Hook environment isolation requires each hook to install its dependencies; initial setup downloads are slow.
Bypassing with --no-verify is always possible; needs CI enforcement to be a hard gate.
best_for: Enforcing code quality, formatting, and linting consistently across all contributors.
Preventing common issues (trailing whitespace, YAML syntax errors, secrets) before they reach CI.
Large codebases with multiple languages needing unified hook management.
avoid_when: The team has strong CI-only quality gates and local hooks add friction without clear benefit.
Hooks are so slow they frequently get bypassed with --no-verify, defeating the purpose.
best_practices: Pin hook revisions to tags (rev: v0.x.y) in .pre-commit-config.yaml; run autoupdate in CI quarterly.
Keep pre-commit hooks fast (Ruff, trailing-whitespace, check-yaml); move slow checks (mypy, tests) to pre-push.
Run pre-commit run --all-files in CI as a required status check to prevent hook bypass drift.
Use the standard hooks repo (pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks) for trailing-whitespace, end-of-file-fixer, check-yaml, check-json.
Add detect-secrets or gitleaks hook to prevent accidental credential commits.
Use stages: [pre-push] on expensive hooks to balance developer experience with quality enforcement.

stacks.name: mypy
strengths: Official Python static type checker; catches type errors before runtime across entire codebases.
--strict flag enables all optional error checks: disallow_untyped_defs, disallow_any_generics, warn_return_any, etc.
Protocol support for structural subtyping: define interfaces without inheritance.
TypeGuard/TypeIs for custom type narrowing functions in isinstance-style patterns.
TypedDict for typed dictionary schemas; Literal types for restricted value sets.
Generics with TypeVar, ParamSpec, and TypeVarTuple for type-safe higher-order functions.
Incremental mode caches results for fast re-checks; mypy daemon (dmypy) for persistent background checking.
Per-module configuration allows gradual adoption: strict on new modules, lenient on legacy code.
tradeoffs: Full --strict adoption on an existing untyped codebase requires significant annotation investment.
Some dynamic Python patterns (metaprogramming, heavy use of **kwargs) resist clean type annotation.
False positives in complex generic code may require type: ignore suppressions with error codes.
best_for: Large Python codebases where type errors across module boundaries are a common failure mode.
Libraries and frameworks where public API contracts must be statically verifiable by consumers.
Teams adopting gradual typing: start permissive, tighten per-module over time.
avoid_when: Small scripts or notebooks where type annotation overhead exceeds the benefit.
Heavy dynamic Python metaclass patterns that fundamentally resist static analysis.
best_practices: Configure mypy in pyproject.toml under [tool.mypy]; set python_version and enable strict mode per module.
Start with strict = false and enable checks incrementally: disallow_untyped_defs first, then warn_return_any.
Use # type: ignore[error-code] for unavoidable suppressions; never use bare # type: ignore.
Use Protocol for structural interfaces rather than ABCs when inheritance is not semantically meaningful.
Use dmypy for fast incremental checks in development; run full mypy in CI on the complete codebase.
Use from __future__ import annotations for forward references and improved annotation performance.
Add mypy to pre-commit hooks with stages: [pre-push] to avoid per-commit latency.

stacks.name: pyright
strengths: Fast static type checker written in TypeScript; ships as the Pylance language server in VS Code.
Strict, standard, and basic modes allow incremental tightening; per-file pyrightconfig overrides are supported.
Excellent Protocol, ParamSpec, TypeVar, and TypeGuard support; first to implement newer PEP typing features.
Local hook integration via pre-commit with no external service dependency.
tradeoffs: Stricter than mypy by default; legacy codebases may see a large initial error backlog.
Some mypy-specific plugins have limited or no pyright equivalents.
Running pyright alongside mypy doubles type-gate overhead with inconsistent error surfaces.
best_for: New Python projects wanting fast, editor-integrated type checking from day one.
VS Code-first teams where Pylance is already the language server.
Projects using Protocol-heavy or modern PEP typing features (ParamSpec, TypeVarTuple, Self).
avoid_when: The project depends on mypy-specific plugins with no pyright equivalent.
The team is already running mypy successfully and migration cost exceeds the benefit.

stacks.name: nox
strengths: Python-native session configuration via noxfile.py; no separate YAML config format.
Isolated virtualenv per session prevents cross-session contamination.
Matrix testing across Python versions or dependency sets with a single command.
Composable sessions: lint, type check, test, build, smoke can each run independently or as a named group.
Integrates cleanly with uv for fast venv creation.
tradeoffs: Slower than running tools directly because each session creates an isolated venv.
Not a build system: no artifact caching or incremental build support.
best_for: Projects needing multi-version Python CI testing (3.12, 3.13, 3.14).
Consolidating lint, type check, test, and smoke steps into one locally reproducible command.
Library and tool projects where compatibility across Python versions must be verified on every change.
avoid_when: The project only needs simple single-version CI; pytest and ruff directly are sufficient.
Build graph, caching, and incremental builds are critical (consider Bazel or Pants instead).

stacks.name: bandit
strengths: AST-level static analysis for common Python security issues: hardcoded secrets, weak crypto, shell injection.
Rule set maps to OWASP and CWE identifiers; findings are configurable by severity and confidence.
Runs in CI and as a pre-commit hook without external service dependency.
tradeoffs: High false-positive rate for B101 (use of assert) in test files; standard practice is to skip B101 in tests.
AST analysis only; does not track data flow, so taint-style vulnerabilities require a SAST tool with dataflow support.
A passing bandit scan is not a substitute for security design review or penetration testing.
best_for: Python projects needing a fast first-pass automated security scan in CI.
Satisfying compliance baselines (SOC 2, ISO 27001) that require automated SAST evidence.
avoid_when: The team treats a passing bandit scan as sufficient security assurance without deeper review.
The codebase is heavily dynamic (eval, exec, metaclasses) where AST analysis adds noise without signal.

stacks.name: pip-audit
strengths: Audits Python environments and lockfiles against the OSV database and PyPI advisory feed.
Accepts uv.lock and requirements.txt inputs; no external service account required.
Maps findings to CVE and GHSA identifiers with fix-version suggestions.
OWASP A06 (Vulnerable and Outdated Components) coverage for Python supply chain.
tradeoffs: Only covers published CVEs and GHSA advisories; does not detect novel or undisclosed vulnerabilities.
Transitive dependency coverage depends on lockfile format; some environments may underreport indirect deps.
best_for: Python projects needing automated supply-chain vulnerability scanning in CI.
Satisfying compliance requirements for dependency vulnerability management (OWASP A06, SOC 2 CC6).
avoid_when: The team conflates a clean pip-audit report with a secure dependency posture; manual review is still required.

stacks.name: MkDocs
strengths: Markdown-to-static-site with a single mkdocs.yml config; minimal setup for documentation-first projects.
mkdocs-material theme adds search, navigation, versioning, social cards, and mermaid diagram support.
Strict build mode catches broken links and missing pages in CI.
Deploys to GitHub Pages in one command; integrates with GitHub Actions for automated doc builds on push.
tradeoffs: Not a full CMS; complex versioned API reference or multi-product doc sites need Sphinx or a hosted doc platform.
Plugin ecosystem is smaller than Sphinx; some specialized output formats require extra setup.
best_for: Developer tools, CLI tools, and planning systems needing clear, searchable, version-controlled documentation.
Projects that want docs-as-code: documentation lives in the repo, builds in CI, and deploys automatically.
avoid_when: The documentation needs complex API reference generation from docstrings (use Sphinx with autodoc).
The product is consumer-facing and needs a CMS with non-engineer content editors.

stacks.name: PydanticAI
strengths: Full type safety through Pydantic: structured outputs, tool arguments, and dependency injection are all statically checked.
Model-agnostic: supports OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Bedrock, LiteLLM, Groq, and 15+ other providers through a single interface.
Built-in evals framework for systematic agent quality measurement and regression tracking.
Native MCP (Model Context Protocol) and Agent2Agent (A2A) interoperability.
Tight OTel integration via Pydantic Logfire for cost, latency, and trace visibility.
Graph support for complex multi-step agent workflows without spaghetti control flow.
Human-in-the-loop tool approval and durable execution for production reliability.
tradeoffs: Newer and smaller ecosystem than LangChain; community tooling and examples still growing.
Logfire observability is first-class but is a paid Pydantic product; OTel backends require extra config.
Graph execution model is more opinionated than raw LangGraph for teams with existing LangGraph flows.
best_for: Python-first teams building production-grade agents with strong type safety requirements.
Teams already using Pydantic for validation who want agent patterns that feel consistent with their existing codebase.
Projects needing structured output validation, tool-calling, and evals in one framework without glue code.
avoid_when: Team is non-Python or uses a multi-language agentic pipeline (LangGraph or Vertex AI Agents may bridge better).
Existing heavy investment in LangChain or LlamaIndex with significant migration cost.
Project requires fully offline/air-gapped operation where Logfire/OTel shipping is blocked by policy.

stacks.name: Vitest
strengths: Single Vite pipeline for dev, build, and test — no separate transpiler config.
Jest-compatible API: drop-in replacement for most test suites with minimal migration effort.
Instant watch mode via Vite HMR; only reruns tests affected by file changes.
Native TypeScript and JSX support without extra config.
Worker-based test isolation prevents cross-file state leaks.
Built-in browser mode for running tests in real Chromium/Firefox/WebKit.
tradeoffs: Browser mode is still maturing; Playwright remains the better choice for true E2E browser coverage.
Best value when Vite is already in the build pipeline; marginal gain for pure Node projects where Jest is simpler.
Snapshot and mock APIs differ slightly from Jest; some community plugins not yet ported.
best_for: JavaScript and TypeScript projects using Vite as the build tool.
Teams migrating from Jest who want faster reruns and a unified pipeline.
Unit and component testing for React, Vue, Svelte, and Solid applications.
avoid_when: Pure Node.js backend projects with no Vite dependency where Jest is already working well.
Need for full E2E browser coverage (complement with Playwright, do not replace).
Project requires Jest-specific plugins with no Vitest equivalent.

stacks.name: Tailwind CSS
strengths: Utility-first classes eliminate context switching between HTML and CSS files.
Design system constraints (spacing, color, typography) enforced through the theme, not convention.
CSS output stays small and stops growing linearly — only used classes are generated.
Built-in dark mode, responsive variants, and state variants (hover, focus, disabled) with no extra config.
Arbitrary value syntax handles one-off values without leaving the utility model.
v4 is pure CSS with no JS build step; integrates with any framework or template language.
tradeoffs: Class attribute verbosity: long class lists on individual elements require discipline or component abstraction.
Conflicting utility classes resolve by stylesheet order, not HTML order — requires careful composition.
Team adoption curve for developers accustomed to BEM or CSS-in-JS mental models.
Does not include a component library; pairs with shadcn/ui, Headless UI, or Radix UI for accessible components.
best_for: Component-driven frontends (React, Vue, Svelte) where reuse happens at the component layer, not CSS.
Teams iterating quickly on product UIs where shipping speed matters more than semantic CSS.
Design systems where token-to-utility mapping provides guardrails without a custom CSS framework.
avoid_when: Project relies on traditional server-side templates (Blade, ERB, Twig) where component abstraction is heavy overhead.
Team has strong BEM/ITCSS conventions already working at scale and class verbosity is unacceptable.
Strict CSP policies block inline style or class generation approaches.

stacks.name: Astro
strengths: Islands architecture: ship zero JS by default, hydrate only the interactive components that need it.
Content-driven focus: built-in content collections with TypeScript type-safety for Markdown and MDX.
UI-agnostic: use React, Vue, Svelte, Solid, or web components within the same project.
MPA-first approach delivers 40% faster load times and 90% less JavaScript versus comparable React frameworks.
Server-first rendering reduces Time to Interactive for content-heavy pages.
Excellent SEO out of the box due to pre-rendered HTML and minimal JS overhead.
tradeoffs: Islands model adds mental overhead when mixing heavily interactive components with mostly static pages.
Not the right fit for SPA-style apps (dashboards, real-time UIs) where Next.js or SvelteKit are more appropriate.
Smaller community and ecosystem than Next.js; fewer ready-made starter templates for complex app patterns.
View transitions router exists but client-side navigation is more limited than full SPA frameworks.
best_for: Marketing sites, documentation, blogs, landing pages, and portfolios where content delivery speed is critical.
Teams migrating from static site generators (Gatsby, Hugo, Jekyll) who want modern TypeScript tooling.
Projects that mix static content with selective interactive islands from multiple UI frameworks.
E-commerce and publishing platforms where SEO and Core Web Vitals are primary KPIs.
avoid_when: Building highly interactive app-like experiences (admin dashboards, collaborative tools, realtime UIs) — use Next.js or SvelteKit.
Team needs a large prebuilt component library ecosystem that assumes a single SPA framework.
Auth-heavy flows with complex client-side session state that benefit from full React hydration.

stacks.name: Clerk
strengths: Prebuilt, customizable sign-in/sign-up/profile UI components that work in minutes.
Multi-tenancy (Organizations) built-in: roles, permissions, and shared resource access without custom code.
Passkeys, WebAuthn, TOTP, SMS MFA, and 50+ OAuth providers supported from the same config.
Billing integration (subscriptions, free trials, waitlists) within the same platform.
SDKs for React, Next.js, Expo, Python, Go, Java, C#, and more; community adapters for Svelte, Hono, Angular.
Session token management, machine-to-machine auth, and backend API protection all handled.
tradeoffs: Per-MAU pricing model escalates for high-volume consumer apps; free tier covers development but not production scale.
Vendor lock-in for user identity data and session infrastructure; migration off Clerk requires user data export and re-auth.
Not self-hostable; teams with strict data residency or air-gap requirements must use Auth.js or a custom solution.
Customization of the full auth flow (complex redirect logic, embedded native mobile flows) requires more work than self-built.
best_for: Developer teams shipping auth in hours rather than weeks for SaaS products.
B2B products needing multi-tenant Organizations with role-based access out of the box.
Consumer apps requiring passkeys and modern MFA without building the infrastructure.
Startups that want managed auth compliance and security without a dedicated identity engineer.
avoid_when: Strict data residency requirements mandate self-hosted identity storage (use Auth.js or Keycloak).
Budget constraints make per-MAU pricing unviable at target user volume.
Need for deep customization of token format, session protocol, or embedded native auth flows.
Team already self-hosting Supabase Auth or has existing identity infrastructure with significant switching cost.

stacks.name: Sentry
strengths: Code-level error context with stack traces, suspect commit linking, and auto-assignee suggestions.
Session replay connects real user interactions to the failing line of code.
Distributed tracing across frontend and backend services from a single SDK.
Release health tracking: crash-free session rate and version adoption visible in real time.
AI agent monitoring (2025): automatic instrumentation of agent invocations, tool calls, handoffs, and token usage.
Cron and uptime monitors track job health and HTTP endpoint availability.
100+ platform SDKs; Python, JavaScript, mobile, and .NET all have first-class support.
tradeoffs: Hosted product cost escalates significantly at high event volume; self-hosted Sentry is complex to operate.
Overlaps with OpenTelemetry for tracing; teams with OTel already deployed may see duplication.
Alert fatigue is real if error budgets and grouping rules are not configured carefully from the start.
Session replay has privacy implications; PII scrubbing rules must be configured for compliant apps.
best_for: Product teams where crash-free rate, error rate, and release quality are tracked as engineering KPIs.
Full-stack apps where connecting frontend errors to backend traces in one tool saves debugging time.
Teams shipping agentic AI features who need visibility into agent call chains and token costs.
Mobile apps where crash reporting and session replay are critical for reproducing user-reported issues.
avoid_when: Pure infrastructure metric monitoring is the need (Prometheus + Grafana is the right layer).
Team has a strict OTel-only policy and budget does not allow a second observability vendor.
App is internal tooling with low error volume where logging is sufficient.

stacks.name: Vue.js
strengths: Composition API (v3) provides React-like composable logic while keeping template-based authoring.
Options API remains supported for teams with OOP backgrounds or migrating from Vue 2.
Single File Components (.vue) co-locate template, script, and scoped styles cleanly.
Progressive adoption: Vue can be dropped into an existing page without a full build system.
Reactivity system is fine-grained and automatic; less boilerplate than React hooks for simple state.
Nuxt meta-framework provides SSR, file-based routing, and full-stack capabilities on top of Vue.
tradeoffs: English-language community and ecosystem are smaller than React; fewer prebuilt headless component libraries.
Nuxt has had major version migrations (v2 to v3) that required significant team effort.
Some enterprise teams treat React as the lower-risk hire-and-staff choice due to market share.
TypeScript integration in templates is improving but still slightly behind React + TypeScript tooling maturity.
best_for: Teams that prefer template-based authoring closer to HTML over JSX.
Progressive enhancement projects where Vue is added to existing server-rendered pages.
Teams coming from Angular who want a simpler reactive framework without the full Angular ecosystem overhead.
avoid_when: Team is fully invested in React with existing component library; cross-training cost not worth the switch.
Project requires maximum access to prebuilt headless component ecosystems (Radix UI, shadcn) that are React-first.
Large team where React developer market is the primary staffing strategy.

stacks.name: SvelteKit
strengths: Compiled framework: Svelte compiles components to vanilla JS at build time, no virtual DOM overhead.
Smallest bundle size of major meta-frameworks; ideal for performance-sensitive and low-powered device targets.
Form actions and server load functions provide clean full-stack patterns without a separate API layer.
Built-in transitions and animations without external libraries.
Runes system (Svelte 5) provides explicit, fine-grained reactivity with less implicit magic.
S-tier developer satisfaction in State of JS 2024 (98% retention).
tradeoffs: Smaller ecosystem than React: fewer prebuilt component libraries, less community tooling.
Svelte 5 Runes is a significant mental model shift from Svelte 3/4; teams upgrading incur a learning cost.
Fewer engineers with existing SvelteKit production experience compared to React or Vue.
Less mature adapter ecosystem for exotic deployment targets than Next.js.
best_for: Developer experience-focused teams who value minimal boilerplate and fast compile-time feedback.
Performance-critical applications for mobile web or low-powered devices where JavaScript bundle size matters.
Small-to-medium product teams who want full-stack patterns (forms, loaders, actions) without a separate API.
avoid_when: Team is React-dominant and cross-training cost outweighs the performance gains.
Project requires a large ecosystem of prebuilt headless components that are React-only.
Enterprise organizations where existing React investment and staffing make switching impractical.

stacks.name: TanStack Query
strengths: Stale-while-revalidate caching eliminates manual useEffect + useState data fetching boilerplate.
Background refresh, window-focus refetch, and retry logic handled automatically.
Infinite queries and pagination patterns built-in without custom state machines.
Optimistic mutations with automatic rollback on failure.
Framework-agnostic: supports React, Vue, Angular, Solid, and Svelte adapters from one library.
Excellent DevTools for inspecting query state, cache entries, and invalidation triggers.
tradeoffs: Cache invalidation logic can be subtle: teams need to explicitly invalidate related queries after mutations.
Adds complexity for simple CRUD apps where Remix loaders, tRPC, or SWR cover the need more simply.
SSR hydration setup requires careful queryClient configuration to avoid data waterfalls.
Bundle size is non-trivial; for minimal apps, fetch + SWR may be lighter.
best_for: React and Vue SPAs with non-trivial server state: dashboards, feed-style UIs, paginated data tables.
Teams replacing scattered useEffect data fetching with a consistent caching and synchronization layer.
Applications where stale data causes UX problems and background invalidation is needed.
avoid_when: App is Next.js App Router with RSC: React Server Components handle data fetching at the server layer.
Using tRPC with its built-in React Query integration (it wraps TanStack Query already).
Simple single-page form UIs with one or two fetch calls that do not need caching.

stacks.name: Auth.js
strengths: Free and open-source: no per-MAU pricing; cost scales only with your own infrastructure.
80+ built-in OAuth providers; adding a custom provider requires minimal config.
Database adapters for Prisma, Drizzle, MongoDB, Supabase, and more.
Fully self-hosted: user sessions and tokens remain within your own infrastructure.
v5 (Auth.js) framework-agnostic: works with Next.js, SvelteKit, Express, Astro, and more.
JWT and database session strategies both supported.
tradeoffs: More wiring required than Clerk: no prebuilt UI, no org multi-tenancy, no billing integration.
Auth.js v5 broke compatibility with NextAuth v4; migration path requires careful session strategy review.
Session management concepts (JWT vs database sessions) confuse developers new to auth.
No native passkeys/WebAuthn support out of the box; requires additional packages.
No MFA support without third-party packages or custom implementation.
best_for: Teams with strict data residency or self-hosted requirements where Clerk or Auth0 are not options.
Next.js or SvelteKit projects on a budget that primarily need OAuth social login.
Internal tools or small products where the free tier and self-hosted model outweigh Clerk pricing concerns.
avoid_when: Product requires multi-tenant Organizations with RBAC out of the box (Clerk is faster to ship).
Team needs passkeys, MFA, or social + password login with a polished prebuilt UI.
Mobile-first apps using Expo where Clerk has a native SDK and Auth.js does not.

stacks.name: Angular
strengths: Opinionated full framework with DI, routing, forms, HTTP built-in
Strong enterprise adoption and long-term LTS cadence
Angular CLI scaffolding and schematics reduce boilerplate
Two-way data binding and reactive forms out of the box
tradeoffs: Steeper learning curve than React or Vue for newcomers
Larger bundle size requires careful lazy-loading strategy
Breaking changes between major versions can be costly
best_for: Large enterprise SPAs with many contributors
Teams that prefer enforced structure over flexibility
Projects needing RxJS-based reactive patterns throughout
avoid_when: Small projects or prototypes where React/Vue overhead is lower
Teams without TypeScript familiarity

stacks.name: Solid.js
strengths: Fine-grained reactivity without virtual DOM gives near-native performance
React-like JSX API with minimal conceptual overhead
Tiny runtime (~7 kB gzip)
SolidStart meta-framework for SSR/SSG
tradeoffs: Smaller ecosystem and community than React or Vue
Reactivity model requires understanding signals/memos carefully
Fewer pre-built UI component libraries
best_for: Performance-critical SPAs needing near-native rendering
React developers wanting fine-grained reactivity without hooks complexity
avoid_when: Teams requiring large component ecosystem (use React)
Projects that need long-term maintenance by large rotating teams

stacks.name: HTMX
strengths: Adds AJAX, WebSockets, SSE to HTML via attributes — no JS required
Works with any server-side stack (Django, FastAPI, Rails, PHP)
Dramatically reduces client-side complexity for server-driven UIs
Sub-10 kB, zero build step
tradeoffs: Not suitable for rich client-side interaction (use React/Vue)
Debugging partial hypermedia responses can be opaque
Limited tooling ecosystem
best_for: Adding interactivity to server-rendered HTML without SPA complexity
Django or FastAPI backends wanting progressive enhancement
Internal tools and admin UIs
avoid_when: Complex client-side state or offline-first requirements
Heavy data visualisations or interactive animations

stacks.name: Nuxt
strengths: Full-stack Vue meta-framework with file-based routing and auto-imports
Built-in SSR, SSG, ISR, and SPA modes switchable per-route
Nuxt modules ecosystem for auth, i18n, image optimisation, etc.
Server routes via Nitro run on Node, Bun, Cloudflare Workers, and Deno
tradeoffs: Magic auto-imports can obscure dependency chains for new contributors
Nitro server layer adds complexity over plain Vite+Vue
Module quality varies; vet third-party modules carefully
best_for: Vue-stack teams needing SSR or SSG with minimal config
Content-heavy sites requiring hybrid rendering strategies
Full-stack projects that want one repo for frontend + API
avoid_when: Team is primarily React-based (use Next.js instead)
Pure SPA with no SEO or SSR requirements

stacks.name: Remix
strengths: Web-standard fetch/form-action model reduces client JS to minimum
Nested routing with loader/action pattern co-locates data with UI
Progressive enhancement by default — works without JS
Runs on any JS runtime (Node, Cloudflare, Deno, Bun) via adapters
tradeoffs: Paradigm shift from SPA mental model; team ramp-up needed
Smaller community than Next.js
Less mature SSG story compared to Next.js or Astro
best_for: Data-heavy apps that benefit from co-located loaders and actions
Teams wanting server-rendered React with minimal client bundle
Edge-deployed apps on Cloudflare Workers or Deno Deploy
avoid_when: Highly interactive dashboards where SPA architecture is more natural
Teams heavily invested in Next.js App Router patterns

stacks.name: shadcn/ui
strengths: Copy-paste component primitives — you own the code, no library lock-in
Built on Radix UI primitives for accessibility compliance
Tailwind CSS styling, easy to customise design tokens
New components added regularly with official CLI
tradeoffs: Components are pasted into your repo — updates require manual re-sync
Tightly coupled to Tailwind CSS and Radix UI
Not a traditional npm package; different mental model
best_for: React + Tailwind projects wanting accessible, styled components fast
Teams that want full control over component internals
Design-system bootstrapping without a bespoke component library
avoid_when: Vue or Angular projects (use radix-vue or similar instead)
Teams that prefer versioned, maintained library updates

stacks.name: Radix UI
strengths: Unstyled accessible React primitives (dialogs, dropdowns, tooltips, etc.)
WAI-ARIA compliant keyboard navigation and focus management
Headless: pair with any CSS approach
Composable API with asChild pattern for flexible rendering
tradeoffs: Requires styling work before it looks production-ready
React-only (Vue users should use radix-vue or Reka UI)
Some complex primitives (e.g. Combobox) missing; need Radix Themes or shadcn
best_for: Design-system authors who want accessible primitives without opinionated styles
Pairing with Tailwind, Stitches, or CSS-in-JS
Ensuring WCAG compliance without writing ARIA from scratch
avoid_when: Need fully pre-styled components out of the box (use shadcn/ui or MUI)
Vue or Svelte stacks

stacks.name: Storybook
strengths: Isolated component development and visual documentation
Supports React, Vue, Angular, Svelte, Web Components
Interaction tests and accessibility checks in the same UI
Chromatic visual regression integration
tradeoffs: Configuration overhead, especially for monorepos
Can drift from production app state if not maintained
Adds build tooling complexity
best_for: Design system or component library development
Co-located visual documentation with component source
Cross-team UI collaboration with living styleguide
avoid_when: Simple apps where a living styleguide adds more overhead than value
Projects without a dedicated frontend/design-system discipline

stacks.name: Zustand
strengths: Minimal API: create a store in ~5 lines, subscribe anywhere
Works outside React components for vanilla JS usage
No boilerplate compared to Redux; no providers required
Supports devtools, persist, immer middleware
tradeoffs: No opinionated structure — large apps may diverge without conventions
Less ecosystem tooling than Redux Toolkit for complex async flows
best_for: React apps needing lightweight global state without Redux overhead
Shared state across components without Provider wrap
Quick prototypes and small-to-medium apps
avoid_when: Large teams needing enforced patterns and time-travel debugging (use Redux Toolkit)
Server state management (use TanStack Query instead)

stacks.name: Redux Toolkit
strengths: Official opinionated Redux setup eliminating most boilerplate
createSlice, createAsyncThunk, and RTK Query built-in
Excellent DevTools with time-travel debugging
Strong TypeScript inference
tradeoffs: Still more verbose than Zustand or Jotai for simple state
RTK Query overlaps with TanStack Query; pick one for server state
Redux mental model (actions, reducers, slices) requires onboarding
best_for: Large React apps needing predictable, auditable state
Teams already invested in Redux patterns wanting modernisation
Complex optimistic update patterns with full rollback
avoid_when: Simple local or cross-component state (overkill — use Zustand)
Server state caching (use TanStack Query or RTK Query but not both)

stacks.name: XState
strengths: Formal finite state machines and statecharts prevent impossible states
Visual tooling (Stately Studio) for designing and debugging machines
Framework-agnostic: works in React, Vue, Angular, vanilla JS, Node
Event-driven model with guaranteed state transitions
tradeoffs: Steep learning curve for teams unfamiliar with state machine theory
Verbose for simple state; overkill for boolean flags
Stately Studio paywalled for teams
best_for: Complex multi-step flows with many states and transitions (checkout, onboarding, auth)
Teams wanting provably correct UI behaviour
replacing ad-hoc boolean flag spaghetti in legacy apps
avoid_when: Simple global state (use Zustand or Redux Toolkit)
Teams without time to learn state machine concepts

stacks.name: Pinia
strengths: Official Vue state management library replacing Vuex
Composition API syntax; stores are plain functions
Excellent TypeScript support with automatic type inference
Nuxt module for SSR hydration built-in
tradeoffs: Vue-only ecosystem
Less mature devtools than Redux DevTools
Less ecosystem documentation than Vuex for legacy patterns
best_for: Any Vue 3 app needing shared state
Nuxt full-stack apps with SSR state hydration
avoid_when: React or non-Vue projects
Projects still on Vue 2 (use Vuex 4 or migrate)

stacks.name: tRPC
strengths: End-to-end type safety from server to client with no code-gen step
Procedures share TypeScript types across monorepo boundary
Integrates with TanStack Query for client-side caching
Works with Next.js, Nuxt, SvelteKit, and plain Node
tradeoffs: TypeScript-only; useless in non-TS codebases
Not a REST or GraphQL API — can't be consumed by non-TS clients easily
Monorepo expected; cross-repo usage is awkward
best_for: Full-stack TypeScript monorepos (t3 stack, Next.js + Node)
Teams wanting type-safe API calls without OpenAPI/GraphQL overhead
Rapid internal tooling prototypes
avoid_when: Public APIs consumed by diverse clients
Mixed-language teams where JS/Python/Go clients all hit the same API

stacks.name: GraphQL
strengths: Client-driven queries eliminate over-fetching and under-fetching
Strongly typed schema is a single source of truth for API contract
Introspection enables powerful developer tooling (GraphiQL, Apollo Studio)
Subscriptions for real-time data over WebSocket
tradeoffs: N+1 query problem requires DataLoader or batching solutions
Complex caching strategy vs. REST with HTTP semantics
Schema design decisions (mutations, pagination) require discipline
best_for: Complex data graphs with many interconnected entity types
Mobile clients needing bandwidth-efficient field selection
Teams exposing one API to many heterogeneous consumers
avoid_when: Simple CRUD APIs where REST with OpenAPI is clearer
Teams without GraphQL expertise; learning curve is real

stacks.name: gRPC
strengths: Binary Protobuf serialization is ~5–10x smaller and faster than JSON
Strong contract via .proto IDL enables polyglot clients
HTTP/2 multiplexing: bidirectional streaming built-in
Code generation for all major languages from proto files
tradeoffs: Not human-readable; harder to debug than REST/JSON
gRPC-web required for browser clients (proxy needed)
Proto schema evolution requires discipline around field numbers
best_for: High-throughput internal microservice communication
Polyglot service meshes needing a single IDL contract
Streaming APIs (server push, bidirectional streams)
avoid_when: Public-facing APIs consumed by arbitrary HTTP clients
Small teams where REST + OpenAPI maintenance burden is lower

stacks.name: ESLint
strengths: Pluggable rule engine for JS/TS linting covering style, correctness, security
Huge ecosystem of plugins (react, import, unicorn, etc.)
Auto-fix mode resolves many issues on save
Flat config (eslint.config.js) modernised in v9
tradeoffs: Config can become complex with many plugins
Slower than Biome/oxc for large codebases
Requires separate Prettier integration to avoid style conflicts
best_for: JavaScript and TypeScript projects needing lint + custom rules
Enforcing team conventions and import ordering
Security rule sets (eslint-plugin-security)
avoid_when: Python/Go/Rust codebases (use language-native linters)
Projects that use Biome as an all-in-one formatter+linter

stacks.name: Prettier
strengths: Opinionated formatter that ends all style debates
Single config; works across JS, TS, HTML, CSS, JSON, MD, YAML
Editor integration (format-on-save) for frictionless DX
Integrates with ESLint via eslint-config-prettier
tradeoffs: Opinionated — some rules are not configurable by design
Slower than Biome; for large codebases consider migrating
HTML formatting can be surprising with whitespace-sensitive markup
best_for: Any JavaScript/TypeScript project wanting zero-config formatting
Teams that want formatting removed from code review
avoid_when: Projects using Biome (which includes formatting)
Python projects (use Ruff format instead)

stacks.name: Jest
strengths: All-in-one: test runner, assertion library, mocking, coverage
Snapshot testing for UI components
Extensive plugin ecosystem (ts-jest, babel-jest, jest-dom)
Parallel test execution with worker threads
tradeoffs: Slower than Vitest for modern ESM projects
Config overhead for TypeScript and ESM (ts-jest or babel-jest required)
Snapshot tests can become noisy maintenance overhead
best_for: Existing React/CRA projects already using Jest
Projects needing broad transform plugin support via Babel
Teams familiar with the Jest API wanting stable, proven tooling
avoid_when: New Vite-based projects (prefer Vitest — native ESM, zero config)
Performance-sensitive CI pipelines running thousands of test files

stacks.name: Hono
strengths: Ultra-fast router using Regex-free trie; benchmarks fastest in class
Zero dependencies; runs on Cloudflare Workers, Deno, Bun, Node natively
Small footprint (~12 kB); ideal for edge functions
Zod-based validator middleware and RPC client built-in
tradeoffs: Less mature ecosystem than Express for complex server-side features
Minimal built-ins; middleware must be assembled manually
Fewer examples and community resources vs Express or Fastify
best_for: Edge API handlers on Cloudflare Workers or Deno Deploy
Microservices needing minimal latency and small bundle
Full-stack RPC with tRPC-style Hono RPC client
avoid_when: Large monolithic server apps with complex middleware chains (use Fastify/Express)
Teams needing extensive third-party plugin ecosystem

stacks.name: Express.js
strengths: Ubiquitous; largest Node.js HTTP middleware ecosystem
Minimal and un-opinionated — full control over architecture
Vast documentation and community resources
Nearly every npm package has an Express middleware adapter
tradeoffs: No built-in TypeScript support; types via @types/express
Callback-based error handling is error-prone; promise wrappers needed
No built-in input validation or schema support
Slower than Fastify or Hono for raw throughput
best_for: Node.js REST APIs where ecosystem breadth > raw performance
Legacy Node servers or brownfield projects
Rapid prototyping with maximum npm compatibility
avoid_when: New projects where Hono, Fastify, or Elysia offer better DX and performance
Edge/serverless deployments (too Node-specific)

stacks.name: Node.js
strengths: Largest package registry (npm) and widest ecosystem
Event-loop concurrency handles I/O-bound workloads efficiently
Same language on front and back end reduces context switching
LTS releases with ~30-month support window
tradeoffs: Single-threaded event loop; CPU-bound tasks block unless Worker Threads used
Callback/Promise async model requires discipline
Module system fragmentation (CJS vs ESM) still causes friction
best_for: I/O-bound web servers and API gateways
Full-stack JavaScript teams (React + Node)
CLI tooling distributed via npm
avoid_when: CPU-intensive workloads (use Go, Rust, or Python with multiprocessing)
Teams preferring type-first language platforms (consider Deno or Bun)

stacks.name: Bun
strengths: All-in-one: runtime, bundler, test runner, package manager in one binary
3–5x faster startup than Node.js; optimised for edge and serverless
Native TypeScript, TSX, and JSX execution without transpile step
Drop-in Node.js compatibility for most npm packages
tradeoffs: Still maturing; some Node.js APIs have incomplete compatibility
Smaller community and ecosystem than Node.js
Windows support improved but Linux/macOS are primary platforms
best_for: New TypeScript/JavaScript projects wanting fastest DX
CLI tools or scripts where startup time matters
Edge functions and serverless where cold start is critical
avoid_when: Codebases with deep native Node.js module dependencies
Production workloads at scale where Node stability & battle-testing is required

stacks.name: Deno
strengths: Secure by default: explicit permission flags for file, net, env access
Native TypeScript without build step
Standard library maintained by Deno team; no third-party stdlib needed
Deno Deploy edge network for sub-10ms global latency
tradeoffs: npm compatibility via npm: specifier is improving but not complete
Smaller ecosystem than Node.js
Different module resolution (URL imports) confuses Node devs
best_for: Security-sensitive scripts and automation needing permission control
Edge functions on Deno Deploy
Teams wanting a zero-config TypeScript runtime
avoid_when: Projects deeply relying on native Node modules without Deno equivalents
Teams where Node expertise and npm ecosystem breadth is critical

stacks.name: Drizzle ORM
strengths: Type-safe SQL query builder; schema defined in TypeScript
Zero runtime overhead — compiles to SQL at build time
Supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and Cloudflare D1
Drizzle Studio GUI for DB inspection
tradeoffs: Younger project than Prisma; fewer integrations and examples
Migrations via drizzle-kit; less mature than Prisma Migrate
No relation-level permission model (handle in app layer)
best_for: TypeScript full-stack apps needing raw SQL control with type safety
Edge/serverless projects where Prisma's query engine binary is too heavy
Projects wanting SQL expertise to transfer directly
avoid_when: Teams that prefer ActiveRecord-style ORM abstraction (use Prisma)
Non-TypeScript codebases

stacks.name: Zod
strengths: TypeScript-first schema validation with static type inference
Composable schema primitives for objects, arrays, unions, transformations
Parse-don't-validate pattern: output is typed, not just validated
Ecosystem integrations: tRPC, React Hook Form, Drizzle, Prisma
tradeoffs: TypeScript-only; no Python/Go equivalent in same package
Large schemas can slow TypeScript checking
Async refinements add complexity
best_for: API request/response validation in TypeScript backends
Form validation in React via React Hook Form + Zod
Ensuring runtime data from external APIs matches expected shape
avoid_when: Python projects (use Pydantic instead)
Simple boolean checks that don't need schema modelling

stacks.name: MongoDB
strengths: Flexible document model; schema evolution without migrations
Horizontal sharding and Atlas cloud platform for managed scaling
Rich aggregation pipeline for complex analytics queries
Atlas Vector Search for AI-powered similarity lookups
tradeoffs: Joins (lookup) less efficient than relational databases
No native ACID transactions across collections until 4.0+
Consistency guarantees weaker than PostgreSQL under some configs
best_for: Document-centric data models with variable schema (CMS, product catalogs)
High-write-throughput IoT or event data ingestion
Prototyping where schema is still evolving
avoid_when: Highly relational data requiring complex joins
Financial transactions needing strong ACID guarantees (use PostgreSQL)

stacks.name: MySQL
strengths: Most widely deployed open-source relational database
Excellent read performance, especially with MySQL 8.x improvements
Broad hosting support (PlanetScale, AWS RDS, Google Cloud SQL)
InnoDB engine provides ACID transactions and foreign key constraints
tradeoffs: Fewer advanced features than PostgreSQL (CTEs, window functions limited in older versions)
JSON support newer and less powerful than PostgreSQL JSONB
Oracle ownership causes concern for some open-source projects
best_for: PHP/Laravel stacks traditionally tied to MySQL
High-read-throughput OLTP workloads
PlanetScale serverless branching workflow
avoid_when: Complex JSON queries, full-text search, or PostGIS geospatial (use PostgreSQL)
Green-field projects where PostgreSQL is equally accessible

stacks.name: Alembic
strengths: SQLAlchemy-native migration framework — uses SQLAlchemy's metadata model
Autogenerate migrations from ORM model changes
Branching, merging, and squashing of migration history
Works with PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, Oracle
tradeoffs: Python-only; tied to SQLAlchemy ecosystem
Autogenerate doesn't catch all schema changes (indexes, sequences)
Migration scripts are raw SQL + Python — requires SQL knowledge to review
best_for: FastAPI or Django (when using SQLAlchemy) needing schema migrations
Python services requiring automated, version-controlled DB changes
avoid_when: Projects using Django's built-in ORM (use Django migrations instead)
Non-Python stacks

stacks.name: Cloudflare Workers
strengths: Runs JS/TS at the edge in 275+ PoPs globally with ~0 cold start
KV, R2, D1, Queues, Durable Objects built into the platform
Free tier generous; pricing per-request is cheap at scale
Workers AI for on-network Llama/Whisper inference
tradeoffs: 50 ms CPU time limit per request (subrequest for long tasks)
V8 isolate environment: no Node built-ins unless using compat flags
Durable Objects add significant complexity for stateful apps
best_for: Edge API handlers needing global low-latency
Static asset delivery and edge SSR (Next.js/Remix/Astro on Workers)
Lightweight data transformation at the network edge
avoid_when: Long-running compute (>50 ms CPU) — use AWS Lambda or Fargate
Node.js-only packages that can't run in V8 isolates

stacks.name: Vercel
strengths: Zero-config deployment for Next.js, Remix, SvelteKit, Astro, Nuxt
Edge Functions and Serverless Functions on same platform
Preview deployments per PR with preview URL
Built-in analytics, web vitals, and speed insights dashboards
tradeoffs: Pricing can escalate quickly for high-traffic or large teams
Vendor lock-in for Next.js App Router features (particularly caching)
Cold starts on serverless functions (not edge) can be significant
best_for: Next.js apps getting the most platform-native features
Frontend teams wanting one-command deployment
Projects needing per-PR preview environments
avoid_when: Long-running backend workloads (use AWS ECS or Railway)
Cost-sensitive high-traffic sites where self-hosting on VPS is cheaper

stacks.name: Pulumi
strengths: Infrastructure as real code (not DSL): loops, functions, classes
Supports all major clouds (AWS, Azure, GCP) and 100+ providers
Type-safe resource definitions with language-native type checking
Pulumi Cloud for state management, secrets, CI previews
tradeoffs: Generated provider SDKs can be large and slow to install
Pulumi Cloud costs money for teams; self-hosted state needs setup
Debugging unfamiliar resource errors requires provider knowledge
best_for: Software engineers who prefer code over YAML (vs. Terraform HCL)
Multi-cloud environments needing a single IaC toolchain
Automated infrastructure testing alongside app tests
avoid_when: Teams with deep Terraform investment and no reason to migrate
Operators who prefer declarative YAML (use AWS CDK or Terraform)

stacks.name: Ansible
strengths: Agentless SSH-based configuration management
Huge module library for OS, cloud, network, and app config
Human-readable YAML playbooks, easy on-boarding
Ansible Galaxy for community roles and collections
tradeoffs: YAML verbosity at scale; Jinja2 templating can become complex
Not idempotent by default: some modules require careful task design
Slower than push-based tools for large inventories
best_for: Server provisioning and configuration management
Deploying to bare-metal, VMs, or on-prem infrastructure
Post-Terraform provisioning steps not covered by IaC
avoid_when: Container-native environments (use Kubernetes + Helm instead)
Immutable infrastructure patterns where config-management is unnecessary

stacks.name: Docker Compose
strengths: Define and run multi-container applications with a single YAML file
Network isolation and volume management without manual docker run flags
Watch mode for live reload in development
Supports profiles for optional service groups
tradeoffs: Not production-grade orchestration (use Kubernetes for production)
v1 CLI (docker-compose) and v2 CLI (docker compose) have subtle differences
Resource overhead on developer machines with many services
best_for: Local development environment mirroring production services (DB, Redis, queue)
Integration test environments in CI
Simple VPS deployments without Kubernetes overhead
avoid_when: Production workloads needing auto-scaling, health checks, and rolling updates
Kubernetes-native teams where Skaffold or Tilt are used instead

stacks.name: Ollama
strengths: Run Llama 3, Mistral, Gemma, Qwen locally with one command
OpenAI-compatible API endpoint for drop-in local dev replacement
Model library with quantized versions for low-VRAM hardware
GPU acceleration on NVIDIA, AMD (ROCm), and Apple Silicon
tradeoffs: Requires local hardware with sufficient RAM/VRAM
Not suitable for production serving at scale (use vLLM or Triton)
Model quality varies vs. GPT-4/Claude for complex tasks
best_for: Local development and testing of LLM-powered features without API costs
Offline or air-gapped environments needing LLM capability
Privacy-sensitive applications where data must not leave the device
avoid_when: Production serving requiring SLA, batching, and auto-scaling
Tasks where frontier model quality (GPT-4o, Claude) is required

stacks.name: CrewAI
strengths: Role-based multi-agent framework; agents collaborate on tasks
Built-in tools: web search, file read/write, code execution
Supports sequential, hierarchical, and parallel crew processes
LLM-agnostic: works with OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, Groq
tradeoffs: Requires careful prompt and role design to avoid agent loops
Less matured than LangGraph for complex stateful workflows
Cost can escalate with multi-agent chains on paid LLM APIs
best_for: Automating multi-step research or content generation pipelines
Prototyping agentic systems with minimal boilerplate
Teams new to multi-agent systems wanting opinionated structure
avoid_when: Deterministic, graph-based agentic workflows (use LangGraph or PydanticAI)
Tasks where a single-agent with tool calls is sufficient

stacks.name: Groq
strengths: LPU (Language Processing Unit) hardware delivers fastest inference available
100–500 tokens/sec for Llama 3, Mixtral, Gemma — significantly faster than OpenAI
OpenAI-compatible REST API for drop-in replacement
Generous free tier for experimentation
tradeoffs: Model selection limited to open models hosted by Groq
Rate limits on free tier; production use requires paid plan
No proprietary models (GPT-4o, Claude are not available)
best_for: Latency-sensitive agentic chains where inference speed matters
Cost-effective LLM inference using hosted open-weight models
Streaming completions in real-time user interfaces
avoid_when: Requiring GPT-4o or Claude's leading model quality
Offline or on-premise LLM requirements

stacks.name: Weights & Biases
strengths: Experiment tracking: log metrics, hyperparameters, system stats automatically
Artifact versioning for datasets and model checkpoints
Sweeps hyperparameter optimisation with Bayesian, grid, random strategies
Weave for LLM evaluation and trace logging
tradeoffs: Free tier has data retention limits; teams need paid plan for long history
Can add latency overhead in tight training loops if not batched
Vendor lock-in for sweeps and artifact storage
best_for: ML training experiment tracking and reproducibility
LLM evaluation and prompt engineering iteration
Teams running hyperparameter tuning at scale
avoid_when: Simple scripts without real ML training loops
Strict air-gapped environments (use MLflow self-hosted instead)

stacks.name: scikit-learn
strengths: Comprehensive collection of classical ML algorithms and preprocessing tools
Consistent fit/predict/transform API across all estimators
Pipeline API for reproducible, leak-free preprocessing + modelling
Excellent documentation and educational resources
tradeoffs: Not designed for deep learning (use PyTorch/TensorFlow)
Single-node only; parallelism limited to joblib
No native GPU support
best_for: Tabular data ML: classification, regression, clustering, dimensionality reduction
Baseline models and benchmarking before deep learning
Feature engineering pipelines in production ML services
avoid_when: Deep learning, NLP transformers, or image tasks (use PyTorch)
Billions-row datasets needing distributed compute (use Spark MLlib)

stacks.name: pandas
strengths: Tabular data manipulation: read, filter, group-by, pivot, merge
Reads CSV, Excel, Parquet, JSON, SQL natively
Rich time-series indexing and resampling support
de-facto standard; massive ecosystem of compatible libraries
tradeoffs: Single-threaded and in-memory; can't handle datasets larger than RAM
Copy-on-write semantics changed in 2.0; legacy code may need updates
Multi-GB DataFrames are slow; use Polars or DuckDB instead
best_for: Data exploration and EDA in Jupyter notebooks
ETL scripts processing medium-sized datasets (< few GB)
Preprocessing pipelines for scikit-learn
avoid_when: Large-scale data processing (use Polars, Dask, or Spark)
Streaming data ingestion (use Kafka + Flink)

stacks.name: FastMCP
strengths: Fastest way to build MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers in Python
Decorator-based API: @mcp.tool(), @mcp.resource(), @mcp.prompt()
Automatic schema generation from Python type annotations
Used as the foundation of the official MCP Python SDK
tradeoffs: Ecosystem is very new; best practices still emerging
MCP protocol itself is evolving; breaking changes possible
Debugging MCP tool calls requires MCP Inspector or Claude Desktop
best_for: Building tool servers that expose capabilities to Claude, Cursor, or custom agents
Wrapping existing Python functions/APIs as MCP tools
Rapid prototyping of AI tool integrations
avoid_when: Projects not using the MCP protocol
Production multi-tenant tool servers (security model still maturing)

stacks.name: Keycloak
strengths: Open-source identity and access management with OIDC, OAuth2, SAML
Rich admin UI for user management, realms, clients, and flows
SSO across multiple applications with single realm
Extensions and plugins for custom authentication flows
tradeoffs: Java-based; resource-heavy — ~512 MB RAM minimum
Steep configuration learning curve for realms and clients
Self-hosted maintenance is non-trivial (upgrades, HA setup)
best_for: On-premise or private cloud deployments with compliance requirements
Enterprises needing SAML federation with existing AD/LDAP
Teams wanting full control over identity data
avoid_when: SaaS products where Clerk, Auth0, or WorkOS is cheaper to maintain
Small teams without DevOps capacity for Keycloak ops

stacks.name: Auth0
strengths: Managed OIDC/OAuth2 platform with extensive SDK and integration library
Actions (serverless hooks) for custom JWT claims and login flows
Universal Login with built-in MFA, social logins, enterprise SSO
Okta backing: enterprise-grade SLA and compliance (SOC2, ISO 27001)
tradeoffs: Pricing jumps sharply after free tier (7,500 MAU)
Vendor lock-in; migration off Auth0 is costly
Less flexible than Keycloak for custom protocol extensions
best_for: B2B SaaS products needing enterprise SSO (SAML) and social logins quickly
Teams without backend authentication expertise
Apps needing compliance certifications without building auth from scratch
avoid_when: Cost-sensitive consumer apps with millions of MAU (use Clerk or Supabase Auth)
Self-hosted/on-premise identity requirements

stacks.name: Snyk
strengths: Scans npm, pip, Maven, Go modules for known CVEs inline in CI
Snyk Code SAST for detecting vulnerabilities in source code
Container and IaC scanning (Dockerfile, Terraform, Kubernetes manifests)
PR decoration with patch PRs auto-generated
tradeoffs: Free tier has project and scan limits
Alert volume can be overwhelming without severity filtering
Some developers prefer OSS alternatives like Trivy or pip-audit
best_for: Teams wanting a single SaaS dashboard for dep, code, container, and IaC security
GitHub/GitLab CI pipelines needing PR-level security gating
Compliance workflows requiring audit trails of security scans
avoid_when: Teams preferring OSS tools (bandit + pip-audit + Trivy can cover the same ground)
Air-gapped environments without internet access

stacks.name: k6
strengths: Load and performance testing with scripted JS scenarios
Built-in metrics (req rate, latency, error rate) exposed as Prometheus metrics
Grafana Cloud k6 for distributed cloud load tests
Browser module for Playwright-like E2E perf testing
tradeoffs: JS runtime is k6-specific; not Node — npm packages not available
Distributed local load generation requires k6 Operator or k6 Cloud
Less feature-rich than Gatling for complex protocol simulations
best_for: API performance baselines and regression tests in CI
Spike and soak testing before major releases
Teams already using Grafana for monitoring (integrated dashboards)
avoid_when: Functional E2E testing (use Playwright)
Protocol-level testing requiring complex binary protocols

stacks.name: Semantic Release
strengths: Automated versioning and changelog from Conventional Commits
Publishes to npm, GitHub Releases, Docker Hub via plugins
No manual version bumping; version is derived from commit history
Plugin ecosystem for Slack/Teams notifications, GitHub PR decoration
tradeoffs: Requires strict Conventional Commit message discipline
First-time setup can be complex with multiple channels
Version logic is opaque if team doesn't follow commit conventions
best_for: Libraries and tools published to npm needing automated release trains
Teams using Conventional Commits wanting zero-friction releases
CI pipelines for packages where every merge to main should release
avoid_when: Apps where release timing is decoupled from commits (use manual tags)
Teams that don't enforce Conventional Commit format

stacks.name: Stripe
strengths: Full payments stack: one-time, subscriptions, invoices, marketplace
Stripe Elements and Payment Links for PCI-compliant UI components
Webhook infrastructure with retry and event replay
Built-in radar fraud detection and chargeback protection
tradeoffs: 2.9% + 30¢ per transaction; expensive at high volume vs. Adyen
Webhook idempotency requires careful server-side event deduplication
Complex for multi-party marketplace splits (Connect)
best_for: SaaS subscription billing with trials, upgrades, and proration
E-commerce checkouts needing global payment method support
Marketplaces using Stripe Connect for creator payouts
avoid_when: Enterprise or high-volume where Adyen's lower fees justify complexity
