Regions where Stripe has limited local payment method support

stacks.name: Resend
strengths: Developer-first transactional email API with React Email integration
Simple REST API with official SDKs for TS, Python, Ruby, PHP, Go
Generous free tier (3,000 emails/month)
React Email component library for building styled templates in JSX
tradeoffs: Younger service; less proven at very high send volume vs. SendGrid/SES
Advanced marketing email features still maturing vs. Mailchimp
Domain verification required; can't start sending immediately
best_for: Transactional emails from Next.js or FastAPI apps
Teams that want to build email templates in React/JSX
Startups wanting fast integration with simple pricing
avoid_when: High-volume marketing campaigns (use Mailchimp or Brevo)
Teams needing deep deliverability analytics (use SendGrid or Postmark)

stacks.name: Twilio
strengths: SMS, voice, WhatsApp, and email (SendGrid) unified under one platform
Programmable Voice with TwiML for IVR flows
Verify API for OTP/2FA via SMS, email, WhatsApp, TOTP
Segment CDP integration for customer journey data
tradeoffs: Pay-per-message pricing adds up at scale vs. direct carrier contracts
SendGrid acquisition means email and SMS are separate dashboards
Regulatory compliance for A2P 10DLC (US SMS) is complex
best_for: SMS OTP and 2FA via Twilio Verify
Voice IVR systems and contact centre applications
WhatsApp Business API notifications
avoid_when: Pure transactional email (use Resend or Postmark)
Cost-sensitive SMS at scale (use MessageBird or direct SMPP)

stacks.name: Capacitor
strengths: Converts any web app into a native iOS/Android app with web view
Native plugin API with official and community plugins for camera, biometrics, etc.
Works with any framework: React, Vue, Angular, Svelte
Ionic integration provides native-style UI components
tradeoffs: Web view performance below React Native or Flutter for animation-heavy UIs
Native module bridging requires Swift/Kotlin for custom plugins
App Store review can be strict about web-view-only apps
best_for: Web developers shipping to iOS/Android without native mobile expertise
PWA-first apps adding mobile distribution as a secondary channel
Ionic-based hybrid projects migrating from Cordova
avoid_when: Apps needing high frame-rate animations or 3D (use React Native or Flutter)
Games or AR apps requiring direct GPU access

stacks.name: React Navigation
strengths: De facto standard routing library for React Native apps
Stack, tab, drawer, and modal navigators with native transitions
Deep linking and universal links built-in
TypeScript-first with typed route params since v7
tradeoffs: React Native only — no web equivalent
v6→v7 migration required TypeScript generics changes
Complex nested navigators can make type definitions verbose
best_for: All React Native apps needing in-app navigation
Expo-managed and bare workflow projects alike
Apps needing deep link routing from push notifications
avoid_when: Expo Router projects (Expo Router is built on React Navigation and is preferred for new Expo apps)
Flutter or Capacitor apps

provisioning_services.name: Supabase
notes: Supabase is a strong default when the generated repo needs managed Postgres, auth, storage, and realtime under one project boundary.
Provision Supabase against the generated project repo only and keep its keys/env vars out of PROGRAMSTART.

provisioning_services.name: Vercel
notes: Vercel is a strong default hosting target for generated Next.js web repos that will be deployed as standalone web applications.
Provision the Vercel project against the generated repo boundary; do not attach hosting config to PROGRAMSTART.

provisioning_services.name: Fly.io
notes: Fly.io is a pragmatic default when the generated repo needs container-based deployment without forcing full cloud-platform setup on day one.
Provision the app boundary and runtime config per generated repo; do not treat fly launch defaults as architecture decisions.

provisioning_services.name: Neon
notes: Neon is a strong default managed Postgres target for generated API and data repos that need a dedicated relational system of record without bundling auth or frontend hosting.
Provision Neon against the generated project repo boundary and write only sanitized connection metadata back into the repo; keep secrets outside version control.

provisioning_services.name: Firebase
required_config: service account or admin credentials for server workflows
notes: Firebase is a high-leverage mobile backend surface when the generated repo needs auth, analytics, notifications, or crash telemetry without a custom backend-first design.
Provisioning is not automated yet because platform app registration, signing, and environment wiring are product-specific and should stay explicit.

cli_tools.name: GitHub CLI
install_methods: Install from https://cli.github.com/ or the platform package manager.
Run gh auth login for interactive auth, or set GH_TOKEN for automation.
notes: GitHub CLI is the lowest-friction default for repo creation, auth, secrets, and workflow inspection in generated repos.
Prefer GH_TOKEN in headless automation and keep tokens out of checked-in config files.

cli_tools.name: uv
install_methods: Install uv from https://docs.astral.sh/uv/ or your platform package manager.
Use uv sync and uv run instead of layering pip, virtualenv, and ad hoc wrappers.
notes: uv is the strongest default Python setup surface for generated repos because it reduces install, venv, and command drift.
Generated Python starters should assume uv-first commands in README and smoke checks.

cli_tools.name: Supabase CLI
install_methods: Install the Supabase CLI as a dev dependency or via the standalone binary; global npm install is not supported.
A Docker-compatible runtime is required for local stack commands like supabase start.
notes: Supabase CLI is useful even when remote provisioning is automated because it bootstraps local Auth, DB, and migration workflows consistently.
Prefer local dev dependency or npx usage so the generated repo carries the CLI version with the project.

cli_tools.name: Vercel CLI
install_methods: Install globally with pnpm i -g vercel or another supported package manager.
Use vercel login interactively or --token in CI/CD.
notes: Vercel CLI is the strongest default for pulling remote env vars and linking local repos to hosted projects.
Prefer vercel env run or vercel pull when you want local parity without manually copying env values.

cli_tools.name: AWS CLI
install_methods: Install AWS CLI v2 from the official distribution for your platform.
Configure profiles with aws configure or prefer SSO and federated auth for team environments.
notes: AWS CLI is the operational default for inspecting identity, networking, storage, and deploy surfaces in AWS environments.
Prefer federated auth and short-lived credentials over distributing long-lived IAM keys.

cli_tools.name: gcloud CLI
install_methods: Install the Google Cloud CLI from the official package for your platform.
Authenticate with gcloud auth login or use workload identity and service-account impersonation for automation.
required_config: Google Cloud credentials or federated login
notes: gcloud is the standard inspection and control surface for GCP projects, Cloud Run, IAM, and service configuration.
Avoid broad service-account key distribution; prefer workload identity or impersonation for automation.

cli_tools.name: Azure CLI
install_methods: Install Azure CLI from the official package for your platform.
Authenticate with az login and prefer managed identities or federated auth for automation.
notes: Azure CLI is the standard operational surface for subscriptions, resource groups, app hosting, and identity-aware platform inspection.
Prefer managed identity and federated workflows instead of embedding service principal secrets in CI.

cli_tools.name: Neon CLI
install_methods: Install neonctl globally or run it via npx/bunx for ad hoc setup.
Authenticate with neon auth or set NEON_API_KEY for automation.
notes: Neon CLI reduces database setup friction after provisioning because it handles project, branch, and connection-string inspection from the terminal.
Context files let developers avoid repeatedly retyping org/project identifiers in local workflows.

cli_tools.name: flyctl
install_methods: Install flyctl from the official Fly.io package or script for your platform.
Authenticate with fly auth login or use FLY_API_TOKEN in automation.
notes: flyctl is the core operational surface for app creation, deployment, scaling, and inspection on Fly.io.
Use it to make deploy intent explicit instead of treating one-time launch defaults as permanent platform design.

cli_tools.name: Stripe CLI
install_methods: Install the Stripe CLI from the official Stripe distribution for your platform.
Use it primarily in sandbox/test mode for local development and webhook testing.
notes: Stripe CLI is high leverage because local webhook forwarding removes a large amount of payments setup friction.
Keep live-mode key operations out of repo scripts; use sandbox defaults until go-live.

cli_tools.name: sentry-cli
install_methods: Install sentry-cli from the official package or binary for your platform.
Authenticate with SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN and configure org/project for release workflows.
notes: sentry-cli is most useful in build and release surfaces rather than local feature work.
Use it when the generated repo needs release tracking, source maps, or CI-integrated error ownership.

cli_tools.name: EAS CLI
install_methods: Install with pnpm add -g eas-cli or use the project-local package manager workflow documented by Expo.
Authenticate with Expo account credentials or CI tokens before build and submit workflows.
notes: EAS CLI is the operational backbone for Expo build, update, preview, and store-submission workflows.
Use profiles to separate preview, internal, and production channels instead of overloading one build configuration.

cli_tools.name: Firebase CLI
install_methods: Install with npm or pnpm as firebase-tools for local and CI workflows.
Authenticate interactively with firebase login or use service-account credentials in CI.
notes: Firebase CLI is useful for emulator-driven local development and deployment of supporting hosting or function surfaces.
Mobile client configuration still needs platform-specific app registration and should not be hidden behind the CLI.

third_party_apis.name: OpenAI
notes: Use Bearer auth with OPENAI_API_KEY and keep the key server-side only.
If the org uses multiple OpenAI organizations or projects, carry OpenAI-Organization and OpenAI-Project headers explicitly.

third_party_apis.name: Anthropic
notes: Direct API requests require x-api-key, anthropic-version, and content-type headers.
Prefer the official SDKs when possible so header/version handling stays centralized and consistent.

third_party_apis.name: Stripe
notes: Use test-mode keys first and separate webhook signing secrets from API keys.
Prefer restricted keys for scoped server integrations instead of broad secret keys when feasible.

third_party_apis.name: Resend
notes: Resend uses Bearer auth and requires a User-Agent header for direct HTTP requests.
Keep API keys server-side; treat sending domains and from-address configuration as part of environment setup, not hardcoded source.

third_party_apis.name: PostHog
notes: PostHog browser setup needs a project token and host; identifying users after login is required for meaningful analytics and replay correlation.
Treat personal API keys as server/admin credentials and keep them out of browser bundles.

third_party_apis.name: Sentry
notes: Prefer Bearer auth tokens over legacy API keys for new Sentry API integrations.
Use the public DSN only in client/runtime surfaces and keep auth tokens for release or admin automation server-side.

third_party_apis.name: Auth0
notes: Auth0 is a strong default when hosted OIDC, social login, RBAC, and tenant-managed auth flows are preferable to building auth directly into the application.
Keep client secrets and management API credentials server-side; public client identifiers alone are not sufficient for privileged workflows.

third_party_apis.name: Clerk
notes: Clerk is effective for product teams that want polished sign-in, session, and user-management surfaces without designing auth UX from scratch.
Treat Clerk middleware and session helpers as application-boundary code; do not let provider abstractions obscure authorization rules inside your backend.

third_party_apis.name: WorkOS
notes: WorkOS is a strong fit when B2B products need enterprise SSO, directory sync, audit trails, and org-scoped identity features without building them in-house.
Model organization membership, role mapping, and SCIM lifecycle rules explicitly; enterprise identity still needs application-level authorization design.

third_party_apis.name: Amazon Cognito
notes: Cognito is a practical default when the product already lives in AWS and wants hosted identity within the same cloud governance boundary.
Keep user-pool configuration, hosted UI policy, and token validation rules explicit; cloud adjacency does not remove identity design work.

third_party_apis.name: Vanta
notes: Vanta is useful when the product needs automated evidence collection, control mapping, and audit-readiness workflows across cloud and SaaS systems.
Treat compliance automation as evidence plumbing and review support, not a substitute for actual control ownership or security design.

third_party_apis.name: Drata
notes: Drata is a strong fit when a team needs compliance evidence collection and control monitoring connected to its engineering systems.
Keep the distinction clear between automated evidence capture and the human review required for control design, exceptions, and remediation.

third_party_apis.name: RevenueCat
notes: RevenueCat reduces store-specific subscription logic by centralizing entitlements and purchase state across iOS and Android.
Client SDK keys are public-facing app configuration; keep secret API and webhook credentials server-side.

third_party_apis.name: Ably
notes: Ably is a strong managed default for realtime channels, presence, and event fanout when the team does not want to operate broker infrastructure for user-facing collaboration features.
Keep publish authority and token issuance server-side; managed realtime does not remove the need for application-level authorization boundaries.

third_party_apis.name: Pusher
notes: Pusher is effective for lower-friction hosted websocket delivery, presence, and channel-based realtime app features.
Treat channel authorization and event naming as explicit app contracts; managed channels are not a substitute for domain modeling.

third_party_apis.name: HubSpot
notes: HubSpot is a practical default when product and go-to-market systems need CRM, marketing, and customer data automation in one vendor surface.
Keep private app tokens and webhook verification server-side; CRM integrations need explicit field ownership and sync rules.

third_party_apis.name: Salesforce
notes: Salesforce is the stronger fit when the business already lives inside enterprise CRM workflows and the integration surface must respect that gravity.
Treat object ownership, sync cadence, and API limit behavior as first-class integration design constraints.

third_party_apis.name: Intercom
notes: Intercom is useful when support, messaging, and lifecycle communication need to show up directly in the product surface.
Keep user identity mapping and message-trigger rules explicit so support automation does not become hidden business logic.

third_party_apis.name: Segment
notes: Segment is useful when the team needs one event contract that can feed analytics, marketing, and downstream customer systems.
Treat tracking plans and identity resolution as explicit product contracts; event fanout multiplies bad instrumentation quickly.

third_party_apis.name: Shippo
notes: Shippo is a pragmatic shipping abstraction when the product needs carrier labels, rates, and tracking without direct carrier-by-carrier integrations.
Treat address validation, label purchase, and webhook handling as operational flows, not just API calls.

third_party_apis.name: EasyPost
notes: EasyPost is a strong fit when the product needs multi-carrier shipping workflows with programmable shipping objects and tracking surfaces.
Keep shipping-state ownership explicit because fulfillment failures are operational incidents, not only API retries.

third_party_apis.name: TaxJar
notes: TaxJar is a pragmatic default for product teams that need sales-tax calculation and filing support without building tax logic directly into checkout systems.
Keep nexus, filing responsibility, and order-source ownership explicit; tax APIs are not policy engines by themselves.

third_party_apis.name: Avalara
notes: Avalara is a stronger fit for larger or more regulated commerce contexts where tax logic, jurisdictions, and enterprise process integration are more complex.
Treat tax classification, exemption handling, and filing ownership as operational responsibilities that sit above the API integration.

integration_patterns.name: Server-rendered product monolith
notes: Choose when a single team can own product, data, auth, admin, and operational workflows together.
Prefer over premature service decomposition when speed and governance matter more than independent deployment.

integration_patterns.name: SPA plus API
notes: Keep the backend API as the system contract and let the SPA stay deployment-independent.
Use when SSR and BFF behavior add little product value.

integration_patterns.name: SSR or BFF web platform
notes: Choose when route rendering, mutation, caching, and auth should be designed together.
Set strict boundaries between server components, route handlers, and external services early.

integration_patterns.name: API plus background workers
notes: Keep request handlers thin and move non-immediate work to jobs.
Do not treat queues as durable workflow state unless the job model truly stays short-lived.

integration_patterns.name: Durable workflow platform
notes: Choose when retries, waits, timers, and state transitions must be visible and durable.
Prefer over ad hoc chained jobs when the process itself is a product concern.

integration_patterns.name: Hybrid retrieval system
notes: Keep source-of-truth metadata and relations structured, then layer lexical and semantic retrieval over them.
Do not let embeddings become the authority model.

integration_patterns.name: LLM-powered agent platform
components: OpenAI API or Anthropic Claude API
notes: Use function calling or MCP tool servers for structured agent actions.
Validate all LLM outputs through Pydantic models before executing side effects.
Start with deterministic tool orchestration before adding agentic autonomy.

integration_patterns.name: RAG pipeline with evaluation
notes: Follow the five RAG stages: loading, indexing, storing, querying, evaluation.
Measure retrieval quality separately from generation quality.
Optimize in order: prompt engineering, then RAG retrieval tuning, then fine-tuning only if needed.

integration_patterns.name: Python CI/CD quality pipeline
notes: Ruff replaces flake8+isort+black as a single fast linter/formatter.
Use GitHub Actions matrix builds for multi-Python-version testing.
Pin action versions by SHA, use OIDC for cloud auth, and never interpolate secrets in run scripts.

integration_patterns.name: Security-hardened API service
notes: Apply OWASP API Security Top 10 as a design review checklist, not just a post-hoc audit.
Use Pydantic strict mode for input validation at API boundaries.
Implement object-level authorization checks in every data access path.
Use Argon2id/bcrypt for password hashing; require MFA for sensitive operations.
Set security headers (HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, CSP) on every response.

integration_patterns.name: Async Python microservice
notes: Use asyncio TaskGroup for structured concurrency: automatic cancellation on failure, ExceptionGroup propagation.
Use asyncio.to_thread for blocking I/O calls (file operations, legacy sync libraries).
Use logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) for hierarchical log namespacing across modules.
Use LoggerAdapter to inject request-scoped context (request_id, trace_id) into all log entries.

integration_patterns.name: Type-safe Python application
notes: Use Protocol for structural interfaces, TypeVar with bounds for generic functions, ParamSpec for decorator typing.
Use frozen=True dataclasses for value objects; slots=True for memory optimization.
Use Pydantic for external input validation at API boundaries; dataclasses for internal data containers.
Use Hypothesis property-based testing for data transformation invariants and serialization round-trips.
Run mypy or pyright in CI alongside Ruff for comprehensive static analysis.

integration_patterns.name: AI agent with prompt discipline
components: OpenAI API or Anthropic Claude API
notes: Use output contracts, verification loops, and completeness contracts for reliable multi-step execution.
Start with reasoning_effort: none/low for execution-heavy tasks; medium/high for research-heavy; tune by eval results.
Use strict mode for function calls and structured outputs; validate all LLM outputs through Pydantic before acting.
Implement tool persistence rules with dependency checks to prevent prerequisite skipping.
Use research mode (plan-retrieve-synthesize) for multi-source evidence gathering workflows.

integration_patterns.name: ML inference pipeline
notes: Use Polars for high-throughput data preprocessing and feature extraction before model inference.
Serve inference via FastAPI with structured Pydantic response models; instrument all calls with OpenTelemetry.
Pin model revisions by commit hash in production; review licenses before deployment.

integration_patterns.name: Full-spectrum LLM multi-agent system
notes: Use LiteLLM as the unified gateway for provider flexibility, cost tracking, and fallback routing.
Use Instructor for structured extraction with automatic validation retry across all providers.
Use LangGraph for stateful multi-step agent graphs with checkpointing and human-in-the-loop gates.
Expose tools via MCP servers for standardized discovery across agent hosts.
Validate all LLM outputs through Pydantic before executing side effects.
Store agent checkpoints and conversation state in PostgreSQL; instrument with OpenTelemetry.

decision_rules.title: Keep authoritative structure above retrieval ranking
decision_rules.when: The product needs recommendations, context lookup, or RAG answers that affect planning or execution order.
decision_rules.prefer: Canonical docs, registry state, typed KB relations, and explicit ownership edges as the source of truth, with lexical/vector retrieval used only for ranking.
decision_rules.because: Authority, sequence, and dependency decisions degrade when embeddings or generic summaries become the truth model.
avoid: Letting vector similarity decide canonical ownership.
Flattening tradeoffs, authority, and evidence into a single narrative blob.

decision_rules.title: Escalate from task queues to durable workflows on stateful process boundaries
decision_rules.when: The process includes retries, timers, compensation, approvals, or human waits across multiple steps.
decision_rules.prefer: Temporal or another durable workflow system instead of stretched queue chains.
decision_rules.because: Long-running stateful business processes need replayable state and visible transitions, not opaque task graphs.
avoid: Modeling multi-day approval flows as chained Celery tasks.
Keeping workflow state only in broker payloads.

decision_rules.title: Prefer Python 3.14 for new async and tooling-heavy services after dependency validation
decision_rules.when: You are starting a new Python service or planning a runtime upgrade and your dependency matrix is green on 3.14.
decision_rules.prefer: Python 3.14 for new concurrency-heavy services, tooling-forward platforms, and runtime research work.
decision_rules.because: 3.14 makes free-threaded mode supported, adds stdlib subinterpreter primitives, improves asyncio observability, and changes annotation evaluation in ways worth adopting deliberately.
avoid: Blind upgrades before validating wheels, CI images, and runtime annotation consumers.
Using free-threaded mode as the default before workload-specific benchmarking.

decision_rules.title: Run weekly deep research as delta maintenance, not open-ended exploration
decision_rules.when: A knowledge domain changes quickly enough to affect stack, architecture, or operational recommendations.
decision_rules.prefer: A fixed weekly review cadence with explicit outputs: change detected, recommendation changed or unchanged, evidence links, and KB update scope.
decision_rules.because: Scheduled research is only valuable if it produces reviewable deltas instead of growing an unstructured archive.
avoid: Appending research notes directly into stack entries without a decision summary.
Mixing speculative signals with shipped production guidance.

decision_rules.title: Prefer explicit LLM routing and typed response validation for AI product workflows
decision_rules.when: The product needs LLM calls, retrieval-augmented generation, or model-facing automation in the primary workflow.
decision_rules.prefer: LiteLLM and Instructor with an explicit retrieval layer such as ChromaDB when semantic retrieval is part of the product behavior.
decision_rules.because: AI-heavy products need model routing, typed validation, and retrieval boundaries to be explicit instead of buried in generic app code.
avoid: Embedding model-provider calls directly across the app without a routing layer.
Treating retrieval as a vague future concern when semantic search or grounding is already a requirement.

decision_rules.title: Prefer durable orchestration for agent and multi-step automation systems
decision_rules.when: The product uses agents, tool execution, or long-running multi-step automation that crosses request boundaries.
decision_rules.prefer: Temporal and LangGraph-style orchestration rather than implicit in-request control flow or ad hoc queue chains.
decision_rules.because: Agent and workflow systems become brittle when state transitions, retries, and tool coordination are not modeled as first-class workflow concerns.
avoid: Keeping agent execution state only in request handlers or transient worker memory.
Treating queue fanout as equivalent to durable orchestration.

decision_rules.title: Prefer RevenueCat for mobile subscription products
decision_rules.when: The product is a mobile app and subscriptions or in-app purchases are first-class requirements.
decision_rules.prefer: RevenueCat, React Native or Expo, and Firebase-backed mobile delivery over a web-billing-only default.
decision_rules.because: Mobile subscription products need store-aware entitlement handling and purchase normalization rather than a generic web checkout assumption.
avoid: Treating Stripe Billing alone as the subscription authority for app-store purchases.
Reusing the web-app default stack without a mobile-specific billing surface.

decision_rules.title: Prefer Yjs plus managed realtime transport for collaborative web products
decision_rules.when: The product is a collaborative web app where presence, shared state, or realtime fanout are core user-facing requirements.
decision_rules.prefer: Yjs with a managed transport such as Ably, plus a web delivery stack that keeps collaboration behavior explicit.
decision_rules.because: Collaborative products need both shared-state conflict handling and a deliberate delivery transport; websocket language alone is too vague.
avoid: Treating generic websocket support as a complete collaboration architecture.
Adding broker complexity before the product actually needs backend event-stream durability.

decision_rules.title: Prefer enterprise identity and compliance surfaces for regulated B2B web products
decision_rules.when: The product is a regulated or enterprise-oriented web app with SSO, audit, or compliance needs.
decision_rules.prefer: WorkOS or similar enterprise identity tooling, plus compliance automation and explicit governance surfaces in the recommendation output.
decision_rules.because: Regulated B2B products usually fail at org identity, auditability, and evidence collection before they fail at basic route rendering.
avoid: Treating consumer auth defaults as sufficient for enterprise SSO and SCIM workflows.
Presenting architecture output without explicit compliance or evidence-handling follow-up.

decision_rules.title: Prefer managed web product hosting and data defaults for generated app repos
decision_rules.when: The product is a web app or mobile app that needs a fast path from planning package to deployable app surface.
decision_rules.prefer: Vercel plus Supabase-style managed app and data services before escalating to heavier platform operations.
decision_rules.because: Generated product repos need clear deployment and app-data defaults faster than they need bespoke platform engineering.
avoid: Starting with Kubernetes or self-managed platform layers before a product deployment path exists.
Leaving web and mobile starter outputs without an explicit managed runtime and data surface.

decision_rules.title: Prefer explicit commerce integration surfaces for product workflows
decision_rules.when: The product needs customer data routing, shipping, or tax behavior as part of its primary workflow.
decision_rules.prefer: Segment, Shippo, and TaxJar style integrations as explicit product dependencies instead of generic custom plumbing.
decision_rules.because: Commerce-heavy products usually need analytics, fulfillment, and tax ownership called out directly so the generated repo can expose those integration seams early.
avoid: Collapsing analytics, shipping, and tax requirements into one generic integration placeholder.
Assuming a payment processor alone covers downstream fulfillment and tax behavior.

decision_rules.title: Prefer explicit experiment tracking and typed data pipelines for ML training workloads
decision_rules.when: The product includes model training, fine-tuning, or experiment-driven ML iteration as a first-class workflow.
decision_rules.prefer: Weights & Biases or MLflow for experiment tracking, Hugging Face Transformers for model loading and fine-tuning, and Polars or pandas for typed data preprocessing.
decision_rules.because: ML training workflows need reproducible experiments, versioned artifacts, and structured data pipelines; ad hoc scripts without tracking tooling create invisible technical debt.
avoid: Running training loops without experiment tracking or artifact versioning.
Mixing untested data preprocessing with model training code without pipeline separation.

decision_rules.title: Recommend status dashboard with Vite + React for API services needing monitoring
decision_rules.when: The product shape is api_service AND the needs include monitoring, dashboard, or analytics.
decision_rules.prefer: A companion status dashboard built with Vite + React, deployed separately from the API service.
decision_rules.because: API services without a visual monitoring surface force operators into raw log tailing; a lightweight dashboard closes this gap without coupling frontend to the API codebase.
avoid: Embedding dashboard routes directly in the API service.
Deferring all observability to third-party SaaS without any first-party operator surface.

decision_rules.title: Recommend web wrapper with Next.js for CLI tools targeting non-technical users
decision_rules.when: The product shape is cli_tool AND the target user is non-technical or the needs include web interface.
decision_rules.prefer: A companion web wrapper built with Next.js that exposes CLI capabilities through a browser-friendly interface.
decision_rules.because: Non-technical users rarely adopt CLI tools directly; a lightweight web wrapper lowers the adoption barrier without rewriting the core tool.
avoid: Rewriting CLI internals as a web app instead of wrapping the existing CLI.
Assuming all CLI users are comfortable with terminal workflows.
match_domains: Developer experience, quality, and supply chain

decision_rules.title: Recommend analytics dashboard for data pipelines needing visual output
decision_rules.when: The product shape is data_pipeline AND the needs include analytics, reporting, or monitoring UI.
decision_rules.prefer: A companion analytics dashboard built with Vite + React or a lightweight charting library, reading from the pipeline's output store.
decision_rules.because: Data pipelines without visual output surfaces leave stakeholders dependent on ad hoc queries; a purpose-built dashboard closes the feedback loop.
avoid: Mixing dashboard rendering logic into the pipeline execution code.
Deferring all analytics visualization to generic BI tools when a focused dashboard would be faster.

relationships.subject: FastAPI
relationships.rationale: FastAPI's boundary validation and OpenAPI generation are strongest when request and response contracts are enforced through typed models.
evidence: FastAPI request validation and schema generation rely on Python type annotations and Pydantic models.

relationships.subject: Temporal
relationships.rationale: Temporal should replace stretched queue chains once a process requires durable state, timers, compensation, or human waits.
evidence: Celery is effective for finite background work but becomes opaque for long-lived workflows.
Temporal keeps state transitions explicit and replayable.

relationships.subject: pgvector
relationships.rationale: pgvector fits when vectors should stay near relational authority, while ChromaDB is better for fast prototyping or smaller standalone retrieval systems.
evidence: The choice depends on whether relational authority or isolated vector iteration is the primary constraint.

relationships.subject: Python Asyncio
relationships.rationale: Async systems become hard to operate without request and task-level logging context.
evidence: Task-aware logging and request-scoped context reduce debugging cost in concurrent services.

relationships.subject: Python 3.14
relationships.rationale: For new services with validated dependencies, 3.14 is the stronger default because it upgrades concurrency and runtime tooling from experimental or partial to more operationally usable surfaces.
evidence: 3.13 introduced experimental free-threaded CPython and JIT work.
3.14 supports free-threaded mode and adds stdlib multiple-interpreter tooling.

relationships.subject: FastAPI
relationships.rationale: FastAPI is the better default when the primary surface is a typed API or automation service, while Django is stronger when the product needs an integrated admin, forms, and server-rendered workflow stack.
evidence: FastAPI minimizes ceremony around typed APIs and async service boundaries.
Django provides batteries-included platform features that reduce integration choices for business systems.

relationships.subject: pgvector
relationships.rationale: pgvector is strongest when vectors should stay near relational authority and transactional metadata.
evidence: Relational and vector data can share one operational boundary when the system of record is PostgreSQL.

relationships.subject: Yjs
relationships.rationale: Yjs handles shared-state conflict resolution well, while Ably is a pragmatic managed transport and presence layer for delivering collaborative updates to connected clients.
evidence: CRDT collaboration still needs a transport and connection-management layer when teams do not want to operate their own realtime infrastructure.

relationships.subject: Kafka
relationships.rationale: Kafka is the stronger fit for durable event streams and replayable logs, while RabbitMQ is often the better default for brokered task distribution and conventional queue semantics.
evidence: The architectural choice depends on whether the product needs ordered event streams with replay or more traditional message-broker routing patterns.

relationships.subject: Segment
relationships.rationale: Segment can normalize and route product events into downstream CRM and marketing systems, while HubSpot remains the customer-ops system where those events become campaigns, lifecycle state, and sales context.
evidence: Customer-data platforms are most useful when they keep instrumentation contracts stable while downstream GTM tooling changes independently.

comparisons.name: Python 3.13 vs 3.14 for production application platforms
comparisons.summary: Python 3.13 introduced important runtime experiments and typing improvements, while 3.14 turns more of that surface into practical platform guidance with supported free-threaded builds, stdlib subinterpreter tooling, annotation evaluation changes, and better operational debugging hooks.

findings.summary: 3.13 introduced experimental free-threaded CPython; 3.14 makes free-threaded mode officially supported and adds stdlib multiple-interpreter tooling such as InterpreterPoolExecutor.
findings.option_a: 3.13: useful for early benchmarking and compatibility scouting, but not the strongest default for production concurrency decisions.
findings.option_b: 3.14: stronger default for new concurrency-heavy systems once dependencies and deployment images are validated.
findings.recommendation: Use 3.14 for new async and concurrency research work; keep 3.13 where dependency support or deployment base images lag.
findings.migration_risk: Medium: verify native extensions, worker models, and runtime assumptions before upgrading.

findings.summary: 3.13 expanded typing features such as defaults, ReadOnly, and TypeIs; 3.14 changes annotation evaluation semantics through deferred evaluation, which can affect runtime introspection and libraries that inspect annotations eagerly.
findings.option_a: 3.13: safer if your framework or internal code relies on current eager annotation behavior.
findings.option_b: 3.14: better long-term default if you validate runtime annotation consumers and want the newer semantics.
findings.recommendation: Treat 3.14 annotation changes as an explicit migration checkpoint for FastAPI, Pydantic, and internal reflection code.
findings.migration_risk: Medium to high for codebases that depend on runtime annotation evaluation details.

findings.summary: 3.14 improves operational surfaces with safer debugger hooks, asyncio introspection improvements, incremental GC, and compression.zstd in the stdlib, while 3.13 was more notable for runtime experiments than for operational ergonomics.
findings.option_a: 3.13: acceptable stable target where teams prioritize package support over newer ops features.
findings.option_b: 3.14: better for platform teams that value debugging, runtime introspection, and newer standard library surfaces.
findings.recommendation: Prefer 3.14 for new platform and developer-experience investments after CI and packaging validation.
findings.migration_risk: Low to medium if your ecosystem is pure Python; higher with extension-heavy stacks.

findings.summary: The main upgrade risk is not syntax but ecosystem readiness: wheels, base images, type-checker baselines, and runtime annotation consumers need verification.
findings.option_a: 3.13: lower ecosystem risk today for teams already running it successfully.
findings.option_b: 3.14: higher near-term validation cost but better default target for new work if green.
findings.recommendation: Gate adoption on lockfile refresh, CI matrix coverage, and a focused compatibility pass for framework/runtime edges.

comparisons.decision: Prefer Python 3.14 for new services and active platform upgrades when the dependency matrix, deployment images, and runtime annotation consumers are validated. Stay on Python 3.13 when compatibility risk is still material or the environment standard has not caught up.

comparisons.name: FastAPI vs Django for product platform design
comparisons.summary: FastAPI is the stronger default for typed APIs, automations, and ML/service boundaries, while Django is the stronger default for workflow-heavy business systems that benefit from integrated admin, auth, forms, and templating.

findings.summary: FastAPI assumes you will compose your own data, admin, and background-work story. Django gives you a coherent batteries-included application platform.
findings.option_a: FastAPI: best when service contracts and explicit boundaries matter more than integrated web features.
findings.option_b: Django: best when the product benefits from a coherent server-rendered and admin-heavy workflow stack.
findings.recommendation: Choose FastAPI for API-first and automation systems; choose Django for admin-heavy business platforms.
findings.migration_risk: Medium when teams underestimate the amount of composition FastAPI requires or the amount of platform gravity Django creates.

findings.summary: Django reduces architecture drift by shipping more defaults. FastAPI gives flexibility, which is valuable but easier to fragment across teams.
findings.option_a: FastAPI: higher composition freedom, higher governance burden.
findings.option_b: Django: lower composition freedom, stronger built-in operational conventions.
findings.recommendation: Use Django when the team needs stronger defaults and fewer platform decisions.
findings.migration_risk: Low to medium depending on team experience.

comparisons.decision: Prefer FastAPI when the product is API-first, automation-heavy, or ML/service oriented. Prefer Django when integrated admin, forms, auth, and server-rendered workflows are core to the product surface.

comparisons.name: Celery vs Temporal for process orchestration
comparisons.summary: Celery is appropriate for finite background jobs and conventional queue-driven work, while Temporal is the stronger choice once the process itself becomes a long-running, stateful product concern.

findings.summary: Celery manages jobs; Temporal manages workflows with replayable state and explicit execution history.
findings.option_a: Celery: strong for bounded async jobs, weak for long-lived multi-step state machines.
findings.option_b: Temporal: strong for durable workflows, timers, compensation, and visibility into state transitions.
findings.recommendation: Use Celery for finite background work and Temporal once workflow state must remain durable and inspectable.
findings.migration_risk: Medium to high if queue-based assumptions are deeply embedded.

findings.summary: Temporal adds conceptual and operational weight but pays it back when process visibility and recovery matter.
findings.option_a: Celery: lighter operationally but easier to let process logic disappear into workers and broker payloads.
findings.option_b: Temporal: heavier platform choice but clearer process ownership and replay semantics.
findings.recommendation: Choose Temporal deliberately when the business process must be visible, replayable, and recoverable.

comparisons.decision: Keep Celery for finite background jobs. Move to Temporal when the product requires durable, inspectable, timer-driven, or human-in-the-loop workflows.

comparisons.name: pgvector vs ChromaDB for retrieval architecture
comparisons.summary: pgvector is the better fit when vectors must stay close to relational authority and transactional metadata, while ChromaDB is better for fast prototyping or isolated retrieval systems that do not need relational co-location.

findings.summary: pgvector keeps vectors near the canonical relational model. ChromaDB optimizes for standalone vector iteration and low setup overhead.
findings.option_a: pgvector: stronger when relational metadata and authority edges already live in PostgreSQL.
findings.option_b: ChromaDB: stronger when you want low-friction prototyping or a smaller isolated retrieval store.
findings.recommendation: Use pgvector when the system of record is PostgreSQL and retrieval should stay close to it; use ChromaDB for lighter-weight standalone retrieval iteration.
findings.migration_risk: Low to medium depending on how tightly retrieval is coupled to relational metadata.

findings.summary: pgvector reduces system count if PostgreSQL is already central. ChromaDB reduces initial retrieval setup complexity but introduces a separate operational surface.
findings.option_a: pgvector: one primary operational boundary if PostgreSQL is already present.
findings.option_b: ChromaDB: simpler to start with but separate from transactional authority.
findings.recommendation: Prefer pgvector for production knowledge systems with relational authority; prefer ChromaDB for smaller prototypes and isolated retrieval workloads.

comparisons.decision: Prefer pgvector when relational authority and vector retrieval belong in one operational boundary. Prefer ChromaDB when speed of standalone retrieval setup matters more than co-locating vectors with canonical relational data.

comparisons.name: Stripe Billing vs RevenueCat for subscription platforms
comparisons.summary: Stripe Billing is the stronger default for web-native subscription products that own checkout and billing directly, while RevenueCat is the stronger choice once mobile store subscriptions, entitlement sync, and app-store purchase normalization become core requirements.

findings.summary: Stripe Billing is best when the team controls the payment UX and subscription lifecycle directly on the web. RevenueCat becomes stronger when App Store and Play billing semantics must be abstracted behind one entitlement system.
findings.option_a: Stripe Billing: best for web checkout, invoices, and direct subscription ownership.
findings.option_b: RevenueCat: best for mobile subscription products that need store abstraction and shared entitlement state.
findings.recommendation: Use Stripe Billing for web-led SaaS subscriptions and RevenueCat for mobile-first or cross-platform subscription products with in-app purchases.
findings.migration_risk: Medium when a product expands from web-only billing into app-store subscriptions later.

findings.summary: RevenueCat reduces app-store billing complexity but adds a separate entitlement authority layer. Stripe Billing keeps authority simpler when stores are not involved.
findings.option_a: Stripe Billing: fewer moving parts when subscriptions live entirely in your own billing stack.
findings.option_b: RevenueCat: lower mobile billing complexity but another vendor boundary to govern.
findings.recommendation: Do not force Stripe alone to own mobile store subscriptions; treat RevenueCat as the default escalation point for store-driven entitlement systems.

comparisons.decision: Prefer Stripe Billing when the subscription product is web-native and checkout ownership stays internal. Prefer RevenueCat when App Store and Play subscriptions, entitlement sync, or cross-platform mobile monetization become first-class concerns.

comparisons.name: TaxJar vs Avalara for commerce tax automation
comparisons.summary: TaxJar is a pragmatic default for straightforward sales-tax automation in product-led commerce systems, while Avalara is the stronger choice when tax classification, jurisdiction breadth, and enterprise process integration become materially more complex.

findings.summary: TaxJar is easier to adopt for common ecommerce sales-tax use cases, while Avalara fits programs where tax calculation is embedded into a broader enterprise-control model.
findings.option_a: TaxJar: better for fast-moving product teams that need credible sales-tax automation without heavy enterprise process overhead.
findings.option_b: Avalara: better for larger commerce operations with more complex tax footprints and integration requirements.
findings.recommendation: Use TaxJar as the default unless tax complexity or enterprise process requirements clearly exceed it.
findings.migration_risk: Medium when products expand internationally or inherit complex tax classification needs.

findings.summary: Neither tool replaces tax policy ownership; the difference is how much organizational process and configuration depth the platform expects around the integration.
findings.option_a: TaxJar: lighter operating model with simpler product-team adoption.
findings.option_b: Avalara: heavier operating model but stronger fit for enterprise tax governance.
findings.recommendation: Treat Avalara as an escalation path, not the default, unless the tax program is already enterprise-shaped.

comparisons.decision: Prefer TaxJar for straightforward product-led sales-tax automation. Prefer Avalara when the business needs deeper jurisdiction handling, enterprise tax workflows, or broader compliance complexity.

comparisons.name: Shippo vs EasyPost for shipping abstractions
comparisons.summary: Shippo is a pragmatic choice for straightforward rate, label, and tracking workflows, while EasyPost is often the stronger fit when teams want a more programmable shipping object model and richer logistics workflow composition.

findings.summary: Shippo works well for common ecommerce shipping flows, while EasyPost is often favored when shipping events and shipment objects need deeper application-level modeling.
findings.option_a: Shippo: better for practical label buying and tracking with less upfront modeling overhead.
findings.option_b: EasyPost: better for teams that want more programmable shipping primitives inside product workflows.
findings.recommendation: Use Shippo as the simpler default unless the fulfillment product requires a richer shipment-domain model.
findings.migration_risk: Low to medium depending on carrier and fulfillment complexity.

findings.summary: Both platforms abstract carriers, but the real decision is how much fulfillment logic the product team wants to keep explicit in its own systems.
findings.option_a: Shippo: good when the application mostly needs rates, labels, and tracking events.
findings.option_b: EasyPost: good when shipping becomes a more explicit operational subsystem.
findings.recommendation: Choose based on the shape of the fulfillment workflow, not feature-list parity alone.
findings.migration_risk: Medium if shipping logic is spread across multiple internal systems.

comparisons.decision: Prefer Shippo for simpler multi-carrier shipping flows. Prefer EasyPost when the application needs more programmable shipping objects and deeper fulfillment workflow control.

principles: Structured facts, ownership rules, and dependency edges should remain authoritative.
Lexical and semantic retrieval should be treated as ranking layers over authoritative structure.
Hybrid retrieval is preferable to vector-only retrieval when compliance, ownership, or workflow order matters.
Two-step retrieval is the default; agentic retrieval should be added only when deterministic retrieval is insufficient.
Optimize in order: prompt engineering first, then evaluation, then RAG retrieval tuning, then fine-tuning only if needed.
Measure retrieval quality (precision/recall) separately from LLM generation quality (faithfulness/relevance).
Use cosine similarity as the default distance metric for text embeddings; L2 for normalized-space workloads.
Validate all LLM-generated outputs through typed schemas (Pydantic, JSON Schema) before acting on them.
Use structured outputs (JSON Schema with strict: true) to guarantee schema adherence rather than relying on prompt-only formatting instructions.
Apply verification loops (correctness, grounding, formatting, safety) before finalizing high-impact LLM actions.
recommended_layers: Document metadata, headings, and structured relations
Vector retrieval with embedding search (cosine similarity, HNSW index)
Optional reranking layer (cross-encoder models for result refinement)
Optional answer validation and grounding checks

embedding_guidance.high_fidelity_model: text-embedding-3-large (3072 dimensions, reducible via dimensions param)
best_practices: Pre-clean text: remove newlines and redundant whitespace before embedding.
Use cosine similarity (equivalent to dot product on normalized vectors).
The dimensions parameter allows trading accuracy for smaller index size.
Chunk documents into semantically coherent sections rather than arbitrary size splits.
Include document metadata (title, section, source) in chunk context for better retrieval relevance.

vector_index_guidance.hnsw: Default for most workloads. Logarithmic search time, high memory. Tune ef for speed vs recall tradeoff.
vector_index_guidance.flat: Best for small collections or multi-tenant setups with <10k objects per tenant. Linear search time.
vector_index_guidance.dynamic: Auto-switches flat-to-HNSW at configurable threshold (default 10k). Good for growing multi-tenant datasets.
vector_index_guidance.ivfflat: pgvector clustering index. Faster than flat for large datasets but requires periodic re-clustering.

search_type_guidance.keyword_bm25: Use when exact term matching matters (code identifiers, error messages, configuration keys).
search_type_guidance.vector_semantic: Use when meaning matters more than exact words (natural language queries, concept search).
search_type_guidance.hybrid: Combine BM25 and vector search for best coverage. Use alpha parameter to weight keyword vs semantic.
search_type_guidance.reranking: Apply cross-encoder reranker on top-k results from initial retrieval for higher precision.

research_ledger.operating_model: Weekly delta review with explicit recommendation changes. Research outputs must separate shipped facts, proposed changes, and unresolved follow-up items.
update_policy: Only update canonical stack entries when the recommendation, tradeoff, or operational baseline changes.
Record version-to-version deltas as comparisons before merging them into broad stack guidance.
Every research pass should conclude with one of: recommendation changed, recommendation unchanged, or blocked pending evidence.
Prefer official release notes, migration guides, and maintained vendor docs over tutorial content.
When evidence is weak or experimental, mark confidence medium or low instead of flattening it into a hard recommendation.

prompt_engineering_guidance.output_contracts: Define exact sections, order, length limits, and format requirements. Apply verbosity controls to specific sections, not globally.
prompt_engineering_guidance.verification_loops: Before finalizing: check correctness (requirements), grounding (context/tool outputs), formatting (schema/style), safety (irreversibility).
prompt_engineering_guidance.completeness_contracts: Track internal checklist of deliverables. Mark blocked items with reason. For batches: determine scope, track progress, confirm coverage.
prompt_engineering_guidance.tool_persistence: Use tools whenever they improve correctness. Don't stop early. If empty results, retry with alternate query/strategy/source.
prompt_engineering_guidance.research_mode: 3-pass: (1) plan 3-6 sub-questions, (2) retrieve and follow 1-2 second-order leads, (3) synthesize with citations.
prompt_engineering_guidance.reasoning_effort: none/low for execution-heavy; medium/high for research-heavy; add completeness/verification first before raising effort.
prompt_engineering_guidance.citation_rules: Only cite retrieved sources. Never fabricate citations/URLs/IDs. Attach citations to specific claims.
prompt_engineering_guidance.empty_result_recovery: If lookup returns empty: do not conclude immediately. Try alternate query wording, broader filters, prerequisite lookup, or alternate source.

openai_patterns: Use strict: true on all function definitions for reliable JSON Schema conformance.
Keep functions under 20 per turn; use tool search for large tool surfaces.
Use namespaces to group related tools by domain.
Use tool_choice: 'required' when at least one call is expected; 'auto' for flexible routing.
Handle parallel function calls: iterate response.output for all function_call items.
Pass reasoning items back for reasoning models (GPT-5+).
anthropic_patterns: Handle agentic loop: check stop_reason for 'tool_use', execute, return tool_result, repeat.
Client tools execute in your code; server tools (web_search, code_execution) run on Anthropic infrastructure.
Tool definitions add ~313-346 tokens of system prompt overhead per request.
Use strict: true for guaranteed schema conformance.

avoid: Treating embeddings as the only context layer
Mixing stale and canonical documents without authority ranking
Adding agentic retrieval before deterministic quality checks exist
Using raw chunk-and-embed without considering document structure and metadata
Skipping retrieval evaluation: always measure recall@k and precision@k before deploying
Relying on prompt-only formatting when structured outputs (JSON Schema) can enforce the contract
Using high reasoning effort as a first resort; add prompt structure before raising effort

coverage_domains.name: Web and frontend product delivery
coverage_domains.summary: Well-covered for SSR, SPA, and backend-for-frontend web products.
current_gaps: Broader Vue/Nuxt and SvelteKit guidance is still missing.
No dedicated browser-extension stack guidance yet.

coverage_domains.name: API, workflow, and backend platforms
coverage_domains.summary: Python and TypeScript backend guidance is mature for typed APIs and durable workflows.
current_gaps: Go and JVM service-stack guidance is still absent.

coverage_domains.name: AI, retrieval, and agent systems
coverage_domains.summary: The KB already covers hosted LLM APIs, agent graphs, retrieval orchestration, vector stores, and structured output tooling.
current_gaps: Self-hosted inference serving guidance is thin beyond Transformers references.
Evaluation tooling coverage should expand beyond retrieval-specific patterns.

coverage_domains.name: Data engineering and analytics
coverage_domains.summary: The KB now covers local analytics, managed warehouses, OLAP stores, and Python-first orchestration well enough to recommend credible default data-platform paths.
current_gaps: Snowflake and lakehouse-specific guidance are still absent.
Airflow and dbt conventions are not yet represented as first-class KB recommendations.

coverage_domains.name: Cloud, infrastructure, and platform operations
coverage_domains.summary: The KB now covers both broad cloud-platform choices and a pragmatic deploy surface well enough to recommend credible hosting and platform-operation defaults.
current_gaps: Cloudflare, Railway, and Render-specific deploy guidance are still absent.
Deeper provider-specific patterns for managed networking, queues, and secret stores are still thin.

coverage_domains.name: Developer experience, quality, and supply chain
coverage_domains.summary: The KB now covers Python, Node/TypeScript monorepos, and compiled-platform build tooling well enough to recommend credible multi-language engineering workflows.
current_gaps: Java and JVM build tooling are still absent from first-class KB guidance.
Package publishing, provenance, and SBOM/signing workflows need deeper coverage beyond baseline CI discipline.

coverage_domains.name: Identity, security, and regulated delivery
coverage_domains.summary: The KB now covers hosted identity, passkey guidance, secret management, and compliance automation strongly enough to recommend real regulated-delivery starting points.
current_gaps: Fine-grained authorization engines and policy tools are still not represented.
Deeper region-specific compliance mappings such as FedRAMP or HIPAA implementation guidance remain thin.

coverage_domains.name: Mobile and cross-platform apps
coverage_domains.summary: The KB now covers mainstream cross-platform mobile delivery paths, but native-first and release-ops breadth is still incomplete.
current_gaps: No first-class SwiftUI or Kotlin Multiplatform guidance yet.
Mobile-specific CI, signing, and store-release automation still need deeper coverage.

coverage_domains.name: Desktop, local-first, and offline-capable software
coverage_domains.summary: The KB now covers credible desktop-shell and local-persistence defaults, but collaborative sync and native-release depth are still incomplete.
current_gaps: Updater, signing, and store-distribution guidance is still thin across target operating systems.
CRDT-heavy collaboration and richer multi-device conflict-resolution patterns are not yet first-class KB guidance.

coverage_domains.name: Realtime collaboration, messaging, and eventing
coverage_domains.summary: The KB now covers event brokers, managed pubsub, and collaborative-state building blocks well enough to recommend credible realtime architecture starting points.
current_gaps: Socket.IO and managed websocket gateway guidance are still absent.
Realtime observability and event-schema governance patterns should become more explicit.

coverage_domains.name: Commerce, communication, and product integrations
coverage_domains.summary: The KB now covers customer platforms, support tooling, analytics routing, and commerce-adjacent shipping and tax surfaces well enough to recommend credible integration defaults.
current_gaps: ERP, billing-ops, and order-management integrations are still absent from first-class KB guidance.
Broader telecom, support-automation, and lifecycle-marketing execution patterns remain thin.
